meta.description: A vendor-neutral, open customer success maturity framework with contextual guidance

principles.title: Customer Success Is a Culture, Not Just a Department
principles.summary: CS fails in a silo. Embed it across every team that touches the customer.
principles.description:
Customer outcomes depend on the product, sales, marketing, and support teams, not just the CS team. When CS operates in isolation, it becomes a bottleneck: context gets lost in handoffs, insights never reach the people who can act on them, and the CS team ends up owning outcomes it can't fully control. The fix isn't better hand-off documents; it's making customer success a shared responsibility. Organizations that embed CS into deal reviews, QBRs, roadmap planning, and escalation workflows unlock great value. Account executives, solution engineers, and product managers should all internalize retention and expansion as their responsibility. The moment CS is excluded from the rhythm of the business, value realization drops and scaling becomes a headcount problem instead of a leverage problem.

The deeper principle is value delivery. Every function that touches the customer either moves them closer to their desired outcome or further from it. The product team ships a feature no one can find; adoption suffers. The sales team closes a deal without verifying fit; churn risk is baked in from day one. The support team takes three days to resolve something the customer needed answered that afternoon; trust erodes. Treating customer success as a culture means each team carries the same question: does what we are doing help the customer get the outcome they came for? The CS team coordinates and amplifies, but the responsibility for delivering value belongs to everyone.
principles.meta_description: A customer success culture treats retention and expansion as cross-functional responsibilities, not one team's KPI. How this principle shows up across maturity stages.
principles.why_it_matters: When CS sits in a silo, context gets lost in handoffs and insights never reach the people who can act on them. Treating customer success as a cross-functional operating principle, rather than a single team's KPI, is what lets the product team prioritize against real usage friction, the sales team qualify for fit instead of just ACV, and the support team surface retention risks the CSM team doesn't see. The teams that scale CS most efficiently are the ones where "who owns the customer" stops being a meaningful question. When every team is oriented around delivering value to the customer, rather than optimising for its own metrics in isolation, CS stops functioning as a reactive rescue function and starts acting as the coordinating layer in a shared delivery system. That shift is what makes scale feel like leverage rather than headcount.

stage_manifestation.crawl: CS is a new or single-person function. The founder or a senior CSM is the voice of the customer in every meeting. There is no formal ritual for sales, product, or support to share what they are hearing. Escalations happen in Slack; QBRs are ad hoc. The culture lives in individual habits, not in the operating system. Delivering value to the customer is something the CS team or founder holds personally; other teams are optimising for their own metrics without a shared view of what a successful customer looks like.
stage_manifestation.walk: CS has a seat in deal reviews and an agreed handoff from the sales team. The product team gets synthesized customer feedback monthly rather than raw tickets. The support team knows which accounts are strategic and escalates accordingly. The culture is starting to be written down: a shared glossary for health, churn risk, and strategic accounts. Some teams are beginning to frame decisions around customer outcomes. The product team asks whether a feature will drive adoption, not just whether it ships on time. The sales team is starting to qualify on fit alongside deal size. The question of customer value is entering the conversation, though it is not yet a consistent input to every decision.
stage_manifestation.run: Retention and expansion goals show up on revenue, product, and support dashboards, not just CS. Cross-functional pods (CSM, PM, sales) run jointly against named account plans. Product roadmaps reference adoption gaps from CS. Comp plans reinforce shared ownership: expansion is a sales-plus-CS outcome, churn is everyone's problem. Customer health becomes a board-level leading indicator and a shared KPI across the product, sales, and support teams, not a metric CS reports in isolation. New hire onboarding across every department includes the customer lifecycle and the organization's definition of a successful customer. Delivering value to the customer is a first-order criterion across every function. The product team evaluates features against adoption impact. The sales team qualifies on fit. The support team measures resolution quality alongside speed. The question of whether a decision helps the customer reach the outcome they came for is present in planning cycles, not just retrospectives.

principles.title: Maturity Requires Iteration
principles.summary: Strong CS teams keep evolving as the business, customers, and product change.
principles.description: CS evolves on two fronts: the way the team runs, and the way the team thinks. Practices, processes, and mindsets all drift if nobody is responsible for upgrading them. The technology landscape, the customer base, and the rest of the company keep changing, which means how CS operates and how CS frames its role both have to change with them. Maturing teams build a regular cadence agreed by the team, preferably quarterly, for evaluating new practices and tools, adopting what fits, and retiring what no longer earns its place. The discipline is in the cadence and in the willingness to revise both the system and the assumptions underneath it.
principles.meta_description: Maturity in customer success requires iteration: revisiting playbooks, tooling, and mindset on a deliberate cadence as the business and the market evolve. How the principle shows up across Crawl, Walk, and Run.
principles.why_it_matters: CS sits at the intersection of product, sales, support, and the customer. Each of those moves on its own clock, and the cumulative effect on how CS should operate is significant over even a single year. Teams that build a habit of revisiting their own playbooks, tooling, and mindset on a deliberate cadence stay ahead of that drift. Teams that do not eventually find themselves running a model built for the company they used to be. The cost shows up in specific places: practices that fit a 20-customer team are still in force at 200 customers, AI tools get adopted by individual CSMs without a clear view on what they should change, and the team accumulates a stack of overlapping rituals nobody trusts. This principle backs the Transform phase of this framework, which presumes a team that keeps iterating on purpose, on both the system and the way it thinks about its role.

stage_manifestation.crawl: The team has not yet asked whether the way CS runs is still the right way. Practices, templates, and tools were imported from a previous company or copied from a vendor blog and have been in place since. New tools get evaluated when a CSM stumbles across one and likes it; abandonment is rarer than adoption, so the stack grows. The team's view of its own role is similarly inherited and goes unexamined: whether CS owns expansion, what counts as a successful customer, how proactive the team should be. Nobody owns the question of whether either the operating model or the mindset still fits the business the team now serves.
stage_manifestation.walk: The team runs a regular retro on the operating model itself, naming what should change and what should hold. New tool evaluations are coordinated through a CS Ops role or the head of CS instead of being left to individual CSMs. Some practices get retired, though usually only after they have visibly stopped working for a quarter or two. The team has started to articulate its own beliefs about how CS should operate, what kinds of accounts get what attention, and where CS adds the most value. There is no formal evaluation process for adopting new methodologies or AI tools yet; decisions are mostly made by whoever has read the most recent article.
stage_manifestation.run: The team runs a regular cadence of operating-model reviews, preferably quarterly, with the same weight on the calendar as renewal forecasts. New methodologies, tools, and AI adoption all enter through a written evaluation flow: defined criteria, a time-boxed pilot with at least one segment, and an explicit yes-or-no at the end. Retirements are announced; practices that no longer fit do not linger as dead code in playbooks. The cultural side of CS gets the same scrutiny on the same cadence: how the team thinks about its role, what assumptions it carries about customers, and how it engages with product and sales. When the company itself changes shape through a GTM pivot or a major reorg, the CS team has a structured way to absorb the implications instead of waiting for the pain to surface in retention metrics.

principles.title: Proactive Over Reactive
principles.summary: Intervene before customers disengage, not after they churn.
principles.description: Reactive CS waits for red flags: a missed renewal, a support escalation, a cancellation request. By then, the opportunity to influence the outcome has already passed. Maturing CS organizations build systems that surface risk early and trigger intervention before customers feel the pain. This means investing in health scoring, adoption monitoring, and lifecycle automation rather than firefighting.
principles.meta_description: Proactive customer success means leading indicators drive action before the customer surfaces a problem. How the principle evolves from Crawl through Run.
principles.why_it_matters: Reactive CS is a tax on the business that grows with customer count. Tickets, escalations, and save calls consume CSM time that could have been spent preventing the churn signal that triggered them. The point of going proactive is not eliminating reactive work; it is changing the ratio. When leading indicators like usage drops, stalled adoption, or executive turnover trigger plays before the customer surfaces a problem, the CSM becomes a trusted advisor rather than a support ticket router.

stage_manifestation.crawl: Most CS work is triggered by the customer: renewal looming, executive change, escalation ticket. The team knows which accounts are in trouble only after things go wrong. There is no shared definition of "risk"; it lives in individual CSM heads. Book-of-business reviews happen weekly or monthly and mostly look backward.
stage_manifestation.walk: A simple health score is live and scored manually on a regular cadence. Specific signals (logins dropped, executive sponsor changed, onboarding stalled) trigger defined plays. CSMs still spend most of their week reacting, but the team can now name three to five leading indicators that precede churn and is building the habit of watching them.
stage_manifestation.run: Health scoring is instrumented and automated. Risk plays trigger from data rather than from CSM intuition. The team tracks the accuracy of its leading indicators and adjusts thresholds quarterly. Proactive outreach is scheduled into the calendar as deliberately as renewal calls. The reactive-to-proactive ratio is a tracked metric the team optimizes against.

principles.title: Segment, Then Scale
principles.summary: Not every customer needs a CSM. Build coverage models that match value.
principles.description: Applying the same touch model to every account is the fastest path to burnout or bloated headcount. High-value accounts need strategic, human-led engagement. Long-tail accounts need digital, automated journeys. The dividing line depends on your economics, and it will shift as you grow. The goal is not fewer CSMs or more CSMs; it's the right engagement for each segment at a cost the business can sustain.
principles.meta_description: A customer segmentation framework matches CS coverage to customer economics. How segmentation evolves from a slide-deck idea into a dynamic operating model.
principles.why_it_matters: Treating every customer the same is the single most expensive decision a CS team can make. A white-glove motion that makes sense for a $500K ACV enterprise account is a margin killer for a $5K SMB, and a digital motion that works for SMB strands the enterprise accounts that need human intervention. Segmentation is how a CS team matches coverage model to customer economics. Done well, it creates room to scale without linearly adding headcount. Done poorly, it stays a theoretical ARR-band slide deck that nobody operationalizes.

stage_manifestation.crawl: All customers get roughly the same motion, usually whatever the first CSM did for the earliest accounts. Segmentation discussions happen as ARR-band slides in strategy decks but do not translate into who CSMs call, what templates get used, or which renewals get joint planning. Coverage is determined by CSM workload, not customer economics.
stage_manifestation.walk: Customers are grouped into two or three tiers (high-touch, tech-touch, digital) based on a combination of ARR and strategic intent. Each tier has a defined cadence, onboarding path, and renewal playbook. CSM book-of-business loading respects the tiers. The edges are still blurry and judgment calls are common, but the principle is in force.
stage_manifestation.run: Segmentation is multi-factor: ARR, product usage, industry complexity, and growth potential combine into a coverage model. High-touch accounts get named CSMs with expansion targets. Tech-touch gets pooled CSMs and structured digital content. Digital-only accounts run on in-product guidance with exception-based human escalation. The model is revisited semiannually against retention and expansion outcomes. As data infrastructure matures, some teams move toward dynamic segmentation: accounts shift tiers based on live signals (expansion propensity, adoption depth, risk score) rather than waiting for the next scheduled review.

principles.title: Outcomes Over Activities
principles.summary: Measure what customers achieve, not what your team does.
principles.description: It's easy to track how many calls were made or how many emails were sent. But activity volume doesn't correlate with customer success. The framework centers on customer outcomes (value realization, adoption, expansion) because those are the leading indicators of retention and growth. A team that drives 10 outcomes with 5 touches is outperforming one that logs 50 activities with no measurable impact.
principles.meta_description: Outcome-based customer success measures what customers achieve, not what the team does. How the principle shifts from activity dashboards to customer-outcome KPIs.
principles.why_it_matters: "We did 400 QBRs this quarter" is an activity metric. "80% of our top-tier accounts hit their first-year adoption milestone" is an outcome. Activity metrics are comfortable because they are easy to hit: hold the meeting, log the touchpoint, send the email. Outcome metrics are the ones that determine whether retention and expansion happen. A CS team focused on activities ends up busy, measured, and ineffective. One focused on outcomes has to be honest about whether the work is moving the customer toward their stated goal, and adjust when it is not.

stage_manifestation.crawl: Success is measured by what CSMs do: number of calls, emails, QBRs scheduled. The team has no shared definition of what a "successful" customer looks like beyond "not churned yet." Customer goals are mentioned at kickoff but rarely tracked against. Activity dashboards are what CS leaders show during executive reviews.
stage_manifestation.walk: Each customer segment has a defined success outcome: completed onboarding, reached time-to-value, adopted core features. CSMs track progress toward these outcomes in addition to activities. Review rhythms (QBRs, internal reviews) start with "where is the customer against their stated outcomes" rather than "what did we do this quarter."
stage_manifestation.run: Outcome attainment is a primary CS KPI. Compensation, promotion, and team-level planning reference outcome metrics rather than activity counts. Plays are designed around outcomes to move, not boxes to check. Activities are still tracked for capacity planning but are not the scorecard. Account plans at this level anchor on the customer's business outcome as the primary measure: revenue protected, efficiency gained, risk mitigated. A productive quarter means the customer hit their goal, not that the team stayed busy.

principles.title: Context Drives Strategy
principles.summary: What works at $5M ARR breaks at $100M. Tailor everything.
principles.description: There is no universal CS playbook. A 3-person team managing 200 SMB accounts operates fundamentally differently from a 40-person team with enterprise logos. Stage of growth, customer segment, team size, and product complexity all shape which practices matter most and which benchmarks to target. Every recommendation in this framework adapts to your context, because best practices without context are just guesses.
principles.meta_description: Context-driven customer success calibrates plays to team stage, segment, and operating constraints. How context shapes strategy at Crawl, Walk, and Run.
principles.why_it_matters: A CS playbook that works for an SMB with a four-person CS team fails when pasted into a 30-person enterprise CS org, and vice versa. What matters is not the specific play; it is whether the play is calibrated to the team's stage, segment, and operating constraints. "Best practice" without context is the fastest way to spend a quarter on a motion that was never going to fit. The framework's stage-plus-profile model exists because the same domain-level gap needs a different response at a two-person SMB team than it does at a fifty-person mid-market team.

stage_manifestation.crawl: The team borrows plays from whatever CS content it reads: vendor blogs, conference talks, whatever the last leadership hire did at their previous company. Fit-to-context is a lucky accident. Strategy is a synonym for "whatever the CSM lead thinks will work this quarter." Playbooks exist only as vibes.
stage_manifestation.walk: The team distinguishes its plays by segment at minimum (high-touch vs. tech-touch). There is a shared awareness that a Fortune 500 customer and a fifteen-person startup should not get the same onboarding journey. Strategy documents reference the team's own constraints (headcount, tooling, data maturity) rather than importing a template whole.
stage_manifestation.run: Plays are explicitly calibrated to the team's stage and the customer's profile. New initiatives include a "why now, why us" section that names the operating context. The team stops adopting practices because they worked elsewhere and starts adopting them because they match the current state of the business. Run teams also update their playbooks based on their own outcomes data. If a play consistently underperforms for a specific segment or motion, it gets revised. Strategy becomes self-correcting rather than treated as a fixed library.

principles.title: Data-Informed, Not Data-Paralyzed
principles.summary: Track fewer metrics, but act on them ruthlessly.
principles.description: Dashboards with 30 metrics create the illusion of insight. Real maturity means identifying the 4-6 metrics that genuinely predict outcomes for your context, instrumenting them properly, and building workflows around them. It's better to act on one trustworthy signal than to stare at a dozen unreliable ones. Start with what you can measure today. Refine as you mature.
principles.meta_description: Data-driven customer success means fewer metrics, acted on ruthlessly. How measurement discipline evolves from spreadsheets to predictive models the team trusts.
principles.why_it_matters: The failure mode is not too little data; it is too much of the wrong kind. CS teams routinely have dashboards they do not use, health scores nobody trusts, and CRM fields that nobody writes to. The point of instrumentation is not the instrumentation itself; it is the decision the data helps the team make. A three-factor health score the team actually acts on beats a 30-factor score that sits in a dashboard nobody opens. This principle is the check against turning measurement into a second job.

stage_manifestation.crawl: Data is spreadsheet-based and often incomplete. Customer conversations happen with gut-feel inputs; the CSM knows the customer is at risk because they feel it, not because a signal surfaced it. The team is aware this is a problem but has not yet built the instrumentation to fix it.
stage_manifestation.walk: Core customer attributes (ARR, contract end date, primary contact, adoption state) are consistently captured. A simple health score with three to five signals scored manually informs the weekly risk review. The team has resisted the temptation to build a 30-factor model before it has evidence the five-factor one is predictive.
stage_manifestation.run: Data flows automatically from product, billing, and support into a single CS platform. Health scores are backtested against churn and expansion outcomes and tuned quarterly. Dashboards are pruned aggressively: if nobody opens it or acts on it, it gets cut. The team has a governance process for adding new metrics rather than accumulating them. When predictive models come online, the operational discipline is ensuring CSMs can reason about them: understanding what a model is flagging, explaining it in customer terms when relevant, and overriding it when the human context calls for it. Trust in the data and judgment over the data need to develop together.

maturity_stages.description: Three maturity stages mapped to score ranges. Derived from capability assessment scores.

stages.label: Crawl
stages.subtitle: Reactive / Foundational
stages.description: CS exists but operates mostly on instinct and firefighting. Customer engagement is inconsistent, metrics are sparse or manually tracked, and most effort goes into reacting to problems rather than preventing them.
stages.typical_profile: Early-stage companies with under $5M ARR, or larger companies that have recently formed a CS function. Team size is usually 1–3 people wearing multiple hats.
key_characteristics: No formal customer segmentation. Every customer gets a similar experience
Health is assessed by gut feel, not data
No documented playbooks or journey maps
CSMs are reactive, responding to escalations and complaints
Metrics limited to basic churn rate, maybe CSAT
CS reports into Sales or Support, low organizational visibility

priority_actions.text: Define basic customer segmentation (at least 2–3 tiers)
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Segmentation lets you allocate limited CS resources where they'll have the most impact on retention.

priority_actions.text: Implement a simple health score using 3–5 signals
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Even a basic health score replaces gut-feel with data, letting you catch at-risk accounts before they churn.

priority_actions.text: Document the onboarding process as your first playbook
priority_actions.why_it_matters: A documented onboarding process ensures consistent time-to-value and reduces dependency on individual CSMs.

priority_actions.text: Start tracking logo retention and GRR monthly
priority_actions.why_it_matters: You can't improve what you don't measure. Monthly retention tracking creates accountability and surfaces trends early.

priority_actions.text: Establish a regular cadence for customer touchpoints
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Consistent touchpoints build trust and give you early warning signals before small issues become churn risks.

priority_actions.text: Get CS a seat at the table: establish regular syncs with Sales and Product teams
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Cross-functional alignment prevents handoff gaps and ensures customer feedback reaches the teams that can act on it.

priority_actions.text: Pilot AI call-summarization in your CSM workflow
priority_actions.why_it_matters: AI summarization reduces note-taking overhead and creates consistent post-call records, freeing time for actual customer work.

stages.label: Walk
stages.subtitle: Informed / Structured
stages.description: CS has organized its data and customers, established basic processes, but hasn't fully operationalized them at scale. The team is moving from reactive to structured, with defined segments and some playbooks in place.
stages.typical_profile: Companies with $5M–$20M ARR that have had a CS team for 1–3 years. Team size is 4–10 people with emerging specialization.
key_characteristics: Customer base is segmented by ARR or tier, engagement models differ by segment
Basic health scores exist but may be inconsistently maintained
Some playbooks documented, especially for onboarding and renewal
CSMs have defined books of business with rough coverage ratios
Tracking GRR, NRR, and basic adoption metrics
CS has a seat at the table but isn't yet driving strategic decisions

priority_actions.text: Operationalize health scores: make them systematic, not manual
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Systematic health scores eliminate CSM bias and ensure every account is evaluated with the same rigor.

priority_actions.text: Build playbooks for risk mitigation and expansion identification
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Playbooks turn your best CSMs' instincts into repeatable processes the whole team can execute consistently.

priority_actions.text: Implement QBR/EBR cadence for top-tier accounts
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Structured business reviews deepen executive relationships and surface expansion opportunities before renewal.

priority_actions.text: Define time-to-value metrics by segment
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Knowing how long each segment takes to reach value lets you set realistic expectations and intervene when onboarding stalls.

priority_actions.text: Start measuring CSM capacity and coverage ratios formally
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Capacity data gives you the evidence to hire ahead of need and prevents CSM burnout from silently eroding service quality.

priority_actions.text: Build a CS roadmap aligned with company strategy
priority_actions.why_it_matters: A strategic roadmap elevates CS from a support function to a growth driver with clear, measurable business objectives.

priority_actions.text: Share customer health data with other teams: create a shared dashboard or Slack channel
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Transparent health data breaks down silos and enables Sales, Product, and Support to act on customer signals proactively.

priority_actions.text: Pilot an AI-driven health score alongside your rule-based one
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Running both models in parallel lets you validate ML predictions against real outcomes before fully replacing the rule-based approach.

stages.label: Run
stages.subtitle: Predictive / Operationalized
stages.description: CS is data-driven and predictive. Playbooks govern the customer lifecycle, health scores trigger automated actions, and CS is a recognized revenue contributor. The team operates with discipline and consistency at scale. Continued improvement within Run is the ongoing work: refining predictive models, scaling AI-assisted workflows, and deepening cross-functional influence.
stages.typical_profile: Companies with $20M–$100M+ ARR that have had a CS team for 3+ years. Team size is 11–25 with dedicated CS Ops, possibly CS leadership at VP+ level.
key_characteristics: Multi-dimensional health scores with automated alerts and triggers
Comprehensive playbooks for all lifecycle stages
Customer journey fully mapped with defined milestones and success criteria
CS actively identifies and drives expansion opportunities
Dedicated CS platform in use (Gainsight, ChurnZero, Vitally, etc.)
CS metrics are part of company-level reporting and board updates
Formal career paths and development programs for the CS team
Customer advocacy programs convert satisfied accounts into expansion and referral opportunities

priority_actions.text: Build predictive models for churn and expansion
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Predictive models shift CS from reacting to churn signals to intervening months before risk materializes.

priority_actions.text: Implement digital CS motions for long-tail accounts
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Digital motions let you deliver consistent value to your entire customer base without linearly scaling headcount.

priority_actions.text: Develop cross-functional alignment frameworks with Product, Sales, and Marketing
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Formal alignment frameworks ensure customer insights systematically influence product direction and go-to-market strategy.

priority_actions.text: Start exploring CS monetization (paid success plans, premium support)
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Monetized CS transforms the team from a cost center into a revenue stream while delivering higher-touch service to customers who want it.

priority_actions.text: Build cohort analysis capabilities for retention forecasting
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Cohort analysis reveals which customer segments retain best and why, enabling you to forecast revenue with greater accuracy.

priority_actions.text: Establish CS benchmarking against industry peers
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Benchmarking gives leadership objective context on whether your CS metrics represent strong performance or hidden risk.

priority_actions.text: Formalize CS integration: include CS in product planning and structured sales handoffs, track customer outcomes cross-functionally
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Formal integration ensures customer outcomes are a shared responsibility, not just a CS team metric that other teams can ignore.

priority_actions.text: Establish a Responsible AI policy for CS: PII handling, human review requirements, and adoption tracking
priority_actions.why_it_matters: As AI tools spread across the CS team, a written policy prevents inconsistent use, protects customer data, and gives leadership visibility into what's working.

priority_actions.text: Build customer advocacy and referral programs to convert your healthiest accounts into a measurable expansion and referral channel
priority_actions.why_it_matters: Advocacy programs turn happy customers into an acquisition channel with lower CAC than outbound sales, while reinforcing the outcomes CS already drives.

assessment_domains.description: Eight capability domains, each with 3 questions scored 1–4. Domain average determines domain maturity. Overall average determines stage.

domains.label: Segmentation & Coverage
domains.description: How you divide your customer base and allocate CS resources across segments.

questions.question: How do you segment your customer base for CS engagement?

options.label: We don't segment; all customers get a similar experience
options.insight: Your team treats every account the same, regardless of value, complexity, or growth potential.
options.next_step: To improve your customer segmentation, start with one dimension: ARR. Separate your top 20% of accounts by ARR and give them a higher-touch engagement model. Even a rough split creates clarity on where CSM time goes.

options.label: Basic segmentation by ARR or contract size
options.insight: You use ARR as a proxy for value. That's a reasonable start, but it misses risk and growth potential.
options.next_step: To improve your customer segmentation, add a second dimension: usage intensity or strategic growth potential. A small account with deep product adoption often needs more attention than a large account on auto-renewal.

options.label: Multi-factor segmentation (ARR + complexity + strategic value + lifecycle stage)
options.insight: You are already segmenting customers by multiple factors, one of the more sophisticated coverage models in use.
options.next_step: Validate that each segment drives a genuinely differentiated CS motion: different playbooks, onboarding tracks, and intervention thresholds, rather than just being a reporting lens.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Verify that strategic value scoring reflects exec-sponsor access and expansion runway, not just ACV. Re-score enterprise accounts annually.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, lifecycle stage is your most actionable second dimension. Make sure renewal timing triggers proactive outreach, not reactive catch-up.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB volumes, four dimensions can create more complexity than clarity. Pick two that drive automated triggers and keep the model lightweight.

options.label: Dynamic segmentation that adjusts based on real-time signals and customer behavior
options.insight: Your segmentation responds to customer behavior in real time, a mature capability few teams have operationalized.
options.next_step: Audit the signal quality: are the behavioral inputs clean, consistent, and acted upon? Dynamic segmentation is only as good as the triggers that power it.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Extend dynamic signals to exec engagement and business outcome tracking, not just product usage. Account health at enterprise scale is multi-dimensional.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Ensure both digital and human CS motions respond to segment changes, not just automated alerts but CSM routing adjustments too.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: Use segment changes to trigger automated journey shifts, not manual CSM reviews. Human re-routing at SMB volumes won't scale.

questions.question: How is CSM workload and account assignment managed?

options.label: Ad hoc; accounts are assigned without clear logic
options.insight: Account assignment is informal, which means CSM workload varies widely and coverage gaps stay invisible.
options.next_step: Define one assignment rule today: group accounts by ARR band and set a rough target ratio (e.g., 1 CSM per $2M ARR in your top tier). Imperfect ratios beat no ratios.

options.label: Basic tiering (high-touch vs. low-touch) with rough account distribution
options.insight: You have a tiered model, but account loads are rough estimates rather than designed capacity.
options.next_step: Document target account counts per CSM by tier. Calculate current actuals. Where you're over-ratio, build a case for either headcount or a shift to digital coverage.

options.label: Defined engagement models per segment with documented playbooks per tier
options.insight: Your assignment model is deliberate. Each segment has a documented engagement approach.
options.next_step: The gap at this level is usually enforcement: playbooks exist but aren't consistently run. Audit one tier's adherence before building out more tiers.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, add an escalation path to your engagement model: which accounts get QBRs, who owns exec alignment, and what triggers a CSM handoff.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, the engagement model should specify when to escalate from digital to human touch. Make that threshold explicit and track it.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB, a documented playbook means a repeatable automated journey, not a CSM-managed cadence. Audit automation coverage, not call frequency.

options.label: Data-driven assignment with capacity modeling, workload balancing, and regular optimization
options.insight: You manage CSM capacity like a resource planning problem. Few teams reach this level of operational discipline.
options.next_step: Turn the capacity model into a hiring signal: when it consistently shows overload in a segment, that's the data point for your next headcount case.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Add outcome attribution to your capacity model: which assignments correlate with expansion and which with churn. Let results inform future assignment decisions.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Extend capacity modeling to digital touchpoints, not just CSM hours. A digital-first tier has real capacity limits too.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: Run capacity modeling on your automation stack, not just human CSMs. Are your digital journeys keeping pace with account volume growth?

questions.question: Do you have defined CSM coverage ratios?

options.label: No; we don't track this
options.insight: Without coverage ratios, CSM workload is invisible. You can't tell if you're under-resourced or over-stretched.
options.next_step: Set one ratio target now: pick your highest-value segment and define a target account-to-CSM count. Track actuals for 30 days before refining.

options.label: Informal guidelines (e.g., roughly 1 CSM per $2M ARR)
options.insight: You have a reference point for capacity, but informal guidelines shift as headcount and accounts change.
options.next_step: Formalize the guideline into a documented ratio per segment. Include it in headcount planning so capacity conversations use the same baseline.

options.label: Formal ratios based on segment, reviewed during planning cycles
options.insight: You have structured coverage ratios that feed your planning process, a solid operational foundation.
options.next_step: Add a mid-year review trigger. Annual planning cycles miss the ratio drift that happens when accounts churn, grow, or shift segment mid-year.

options.label: Ratios are regularly reviewed, adjusted based on journey complexity, and modeled against outcomes
options.insight: Coverage ratios are a living model for you, adjusted by complexity and validated against retention outcomes.
options.next_step: Publish ratio benchmarks internally so sales and support leaders understand how CS capacity is allocated and can flag when it's under pressure.

domains.label: Journey & Lifecycle
domains.description: How well you've mapped, documented, and operationalized the customer journey from onboarding through renewal and expansion.

questions.question: Is your customer journey mapped?

options.label: No; we don't have a documented customer journey
options.insight: Your team navigates every customer relationship from memory. There's no shared map of what good looks like.
options.next_step: Start with a rough sketch: list the 5–7 moments that matter most (handoff → onboarding → first value → adoption → renewal). A whiteboard is enough. Get it shared before perfecting it.

options.label: Partially mapped for some segments or lifecycle stages
options.insight: You have a partial map: coverage exists for some customers but not consistently across all segments.
options.next_step: Identify which segment has the most complete journey documentation, then extend it to the next. Don't start over. Fill the gaps in what already exists.

options.label: Fully mapped with defined stages, milestones, and success criteria
options.insight: You have a full lifecycle map with defined stages, milestones, and success criteria, a solid operational foundation.
options.next_step: Validate the map against real customer behavior: does the journey your team documented match how customers actually move through the product? Discrepancies reveal where CSM effort is misdirected.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, validate that success criteria reflect the customer's business outcomes, not just product adoption milestones. Run one account through the map as a test case.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, check whether your mapped milestones are specific enough to trigger automated alerts. Vague milestones don't support lifecycle automation.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, validate that the journey map is simple enough to run without CSM intervention at each stage. Complexity that requires human judgment won't scale.

options.label: Mapped with automated triggers, milestone tracking, and continuous optimization based on data
options.insight: Your journey map is a living operational system: automated, measured, and continuously refined.
options.next_step: The next lever is predictive: use milestone velocity (how fast customers reach each stage) to identify accounts drifting off-track before they signal risk.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: At enterprise, add stakeholder-level milestone tracking. User adoption tells you one story, but exec engagement tells you whether the value story is landing at the right level.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, use milestone velocity to dynamically trigger CSM intervention: accounts moving slower than their cohort deserve a human touch before they become at-risk.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB, milestone velocity powers automated re-engagement. Slow progress triggers an in-app nudge or automated email, not a CSM call.

questions.question: Do you have playbooks for key customer lifecycle moments?

options.label: No playbooks; CSMs handle situations individually
options.insight: Every CSM is improvising. The quality of your customers' experience depends on which CSM they got.
options.next_step: Write one playbook this week: pick your most common risk signal and document exactly what to do. A single shared playbook removes the inconsistency that costs you renewals.

options.label: A few informal playbooks (e.g., onboarding checklist)
options.insight: You have the seed of a playbook library: informal guides that individual CSMs probably execute differently.
options.next_step: Formalize what you have: pick the 2–3 playbooks used most often, document the exact steps, assign owners to each stage, and review adherence once a week.

options.label: Documented playbooks for major lifecycle moments (onboarding, risk, renewal, expansion)
options.insight: You have a documented library covering the lifecycle moments that drive outcomes, a meaningful operational advantage.
options.next_step: Audit adherence, not content. The limiting factor at this stage is usually that playbooks exist but CSMs run them inconsistently. Check one playbook's completion rate before adding new ones.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, add a QBR playbook and an exec-engagement renewal playbook if you don't have them. These moments disproportionately determine renewal outcomes at enterprise scale.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, focus on the expansion playbook. Upsell timing and qualification criteria are the gaps most mid-market teams leave undocumented.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB, the most impactful missing playbook is usually digital re-engagement: what automated action fires when an account goes dark for 14 days?

options.label: Comprehensive playbooks that are segment-specific, regularly tested, and updated based on outcomes
options.insight: Your playbook library is segment-specific, outcome-tested, and actively maintained, a genuinely mature capability.
options.next_step: Run a cross-segment playbook review: which plays consistently drive the best outcomes? Identify the top 2 and increase their use. Retire the ones that never get completed.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: At enterprise scale, assign each major playbook a DRI who reviews outcomes quarterly and proposes updates. Playbooks that nobody owns quietly become stale.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, pilot your top-performing playbook in a digital format. Can it run with less CSM involvement? That's your path to scale.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB, the best playbooks run as automated journeys. Check whether your top-outcome playbook could run entirely without CSM input.

questions.question: How is customer onboarding managed?

options.label: Ad hoc; each CSM handles it differently
options.insight: Onboarding is improvised, which means time-to-value varies widely and customers can't predict their experience.
options.next_step: Create a standard onboarding template this week: 5–7 steps, a clear owner for each, and a target timeline. Adopt it for every new customer immediately, don't wait for it to be perfect.

options.label: Basic checklist or template exists
options.insight: You have an onboarding template, but it's probably inconsistently followed and doesn't measure outcomes.
options.next_step: Add two things to your existing template: a time-to-first-value target and a mandatory milestone sign-off. Measuring these forces both customer and CSM accountability.

options.label: Structured program with milestones, time-to-value targets, and handoff criteria
options.insight: Your onboarding is structured and measured. Most teams never get past the checklist stage.
options.next_step: The gap at this level is usually the handoff to ongoing CS: does your CSM have full context at the moment they take over? If the handoff relies on memory or Slack, build a structured handoff document.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, build an exec check-in into week 4 of onboarding: that's when the buyer evaluates whether the purchase is delivering. Missing it is the most common enterprise onboarding failure.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, focus on the handoff to ongoing CS: build a structured account brief that transfers context from implementation to the CSM without requiring them to re-interview the customer.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB, optimize for time-to-first-value, not onboarding completeness. An SMB customer who reaches their first 'aha moment' in week 1 has a fundamentally different retention trajectory.

options.label: Segment-specific onboarding programs with measured outcomes, automation, and continuous improvement
options.insight: You run segment-specific, automated, and continuously improved onboarding, among the most mature CS capabilities in practice.
options.next_step: Benchmark onboarding outcomes across cohorts: do customers onboarded in Q1 retain better than Q3? Cohort analysis catches seasonal or team-driven drift before it compounds.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: At enterprise, correlate onboarding outcomes with year-1 expansion. Accounts that reach value faster expand more, so make this connection visible in your data and use it to justify onboarding investment.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, use cohort analysis to identify which onboarding tracks produce the best 12-month GRR. If one segment consistently outperforms, export that approach to the others.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB, connect onboarding completion rate to product activation. Accounts that churn at month 3 are usually the ones that never finished onboarding, so make the correlation visible.

domains.label: Health Scoring & Risk
domains.description: How you assess customer health, detect risk signals, and manage at-risk accounts.

questions.question: Do you have customer health scores?

options.label: No; health is assessed informally by CSM judgment
options.insight: Your health signal is entirely human: fast but not scalable, and invisible to anyone outside the CSM.
options.next_step: Start with a 3-signal spreadsheet RAG model: usage (product logins), support (open tickets), engagement (last CSM contact). Run it for 60 days before investing in tooling.

options.label: Basic health score exists but is manually maintained (e.g., spreadsheet RAG status)
options.insight: You have a health framework in place. The gap is that manual maintenance limits its timeliness and coverage.
options.next_step: Identify one or two data sources (product usage API, CRM open tickets) you can automate first. Even partial automation beats a fully manual score.

options.label: Multi-dimensional health score combining usage, engagement, support, and sentiment data
options.insight: A multi-dimensional health score puts you ahead of most teams. The model covers the signals that matter.
options.next_step: Audit whether each dimension carries predictive weight. Drop signals that don't correlate with churn or renewal in your cohort data. Fewer meaningful signals outperform many noisy ones.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, add an executive engagement dimension. Loss of executive sponsor access predicts churn earlier than usage drops, so make it a scored signal.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, add renewal timing as a weight modifier. A yellow health score at T-90 days should auto-elevate to red to give recovery time.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, simplify to the two or three signals with the highest churn predictiveness. Run a quick logistic regression on historical data to identify them.

options.label: Predictive health scoring with automated risk triggers and ML-based signals
options.insight: Predictive health scoring with automation is the ceiling of what CS tooling can do. You have a mature foundation here.
options.next_step: Validate your model's precision and recall quarterly. Adjust trigger thresholds as your product and customer mix evolves to prevent alert fatigue.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Layer qualitative signals (executive sponsor sentiment, procurement relationship health) that your ML model cannot see in product data.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Ensure your automated risk triggers route correctly at scale. Mid-market volume means a false-positive spike can overwhelm CSM capacity quickly.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: Shift investment toward automated responses, not just automated detection. At SMB volumes, human follow-up on every flag is not sustainable.

questions.question: How do you identify at-risk accounts?

options.label: Reactively, when customers complain or announce they're leaving
options.insight: Identifying risk only when customers surface it means you're always responding to the last stage of churn, not the first signals.
options.next_step: Define three early-warning signals from existing data (e.g., no login in 14 days, 3+ open support tickets, no QBR completed). Flag these manually until you can automate them.

options.label: CSM gut feel and manual monitoring of key accounts
options.insight: Gut feel works for your top accounts but leaves risk invisible in the long tail: the accounts no CSM is actively watching.
options.next_step: Document the signals your best CSMs already watch intuitively. Turn those into a shared checklist applied to all accounts, then automate the checklist.

options.label: Data-driven signals with defined risk thresholds and escalation paths
options.insight: Defined thresholds and escalation paths turn risk identification from art into process, a significant operational milestone.
options.next_step: Backtest your thresholds against the last 12 months of churn. If current thresholds would have caught 70%+ of churn with 6 weeks of lead time, they are well calibrated.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, add a qualitative override layer. Quantitative signals miss relationship risk, so add a periodic CSM judgment check alongside automated flags.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, verify your escalation path triggers fast enough. A risk flag that takes 2 weeks to reach the right person has little recovery value.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, risk identification is only useful if it triggers an automated response. Build the response into the threshold trigger, not a manual handoff.

options.label: Predictive models that flag risk early, with automated escalation and cross-functional response
options.insight: Automated cross-functional risk response is rare. Most teams identify risk but leave action coordination entirely to the CSM.
options.next_step: Track how long it takes from flagging a risk to taking the first action. If that gap is regularly more than 48 hours, find the bottleneck in your escalation path and fix it.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure cross-functional escalation includes a named executive sponsor relationship owner. Enterprise risk response requires C-suite involvement protocols.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Add a feedback loop: track which automated interventions actually moved accounts from red to green. Kill the interventions that consistently don't move the needle.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, the value of predictive models is triggering automated plays, not human escalations. Verify save plays are fully automated end-to-end.

questions.question: How are at-risk accounts managed once identified?

options.label: No formal process; CSM handles it alone
options.insight: Without a structured save process, outcomes depend entirely on individual CSM skill, which means they vary widely and don't improve systematically.
options.next_step: Write a one-page save playbook: who gets involved, when to escalate to manager, what the first 5 actions are. Apply it to your next 3 at-risk accounts and refine from results.

options.label: CSM escalates to manager, response is case-by-case
options.insight: Manager escalation is a meaningful step, so you have a safety net. But case-by-case responses mean you're reinventing the approach every time.
options.next_step: Document your last 5 save attempts: what worked, what didn't, how long each took. Use those to draft a tiered response playbook with defined owner-by-tier criteria.

options.label: Defined save playbook with escalation path, executive involvement criteria, and tracking
options.insight: A documented save playbook with tracked outcomes means your risk management gets better over time, not just faster.
options.next_step: Analyze your playbook's win rate by account segment and churn reason. If win rate varies significantly by category, you have segment-specific plays to build.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Add formal executive sponsor reconnection as a required playbook step. Executive-level intervention has the highest recovery rate for enterprise churn, so make it non-optional.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Formalize the criteria for product or pricing concessions. Undefined boundaries lead to inconsistent offers and margin erosion at mid-market scale.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, identify which playbook steps can be automated (triggered emails, usage-recovery guides) vs. which genuinely require human contact. Automate first.

options.label: Cross-functional risk response team with documented save plays, win-back programs, and outcome analysis
options.insight: Cross-functional risk response with outcome analysis is the operational ceiling. You can improve and measure save effectiveness systematically over time.
options.next_step: Run a quarterly post-mortem on churn cases: which were salvageable but slipped through, which were lost too early to save? Use findings to recalibrate early-warning thresholds.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Invest in win-back programs for enterprise. Lost enterprise logos often return within 18-24 months if the relationship stayed intact, and structured win-back has strong ROI.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Test whether win-back programs are cost-effective at your ACV level. Quantify recovery savings vs. new-logo acquisition cost before scaling the program.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, win-back programs should be fully automated: triggered email sequences, self-serve reactivation, no manual CSM touch. Measure reactivation rate vs. program cost.

domains.label: Metrics & Data
domains.description: What you measure, how you measure it, and the tooling and infrastructure supporting your CS analytics.

questions.question: What retention and revenue metrics do you track?

options.label: Basic churn count or rate only
options.insight: Churn rate alone is a lagging indicator with no signal about where revenue risk concentrates or why.
options.next_step: Add gross revenue retention (GRR) as the next metric. GRR shows whether revenue from retained customers is eroding before NRR expansion masks it.

options.label: Logo retention and revenue retention (GRR or NRR)
options.insight: Logo and revenue retention gives you two dimensions (customer count and revenue health): a solid operational baseline.
options.next_step: Separate GRR from NRR if you're tracking them as one number. The gap between them is your expansion effectiveness, and tracking both reveals which lever matters more.

options.label: GRR and NRR tracked separately, with cohort-level visibility
options.insight: Cohort-level GRR and NRR is a sophisticated setup. Very few teams at mid-maturity have this level of retention granularity.
options.next_step: Extend cohort analysis to segment by acquisition source, not just time period. Customers from different channels often show structurally different retention curves.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Overlay cohort data with executive sponsor tenure. Loss of sponsor access correlates with retention cohort breaks, so tracking it reveals a leading indicator hidden in your existing data.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Use your cohort data to set internal benchmarks rather than relying on industry averages. Your product and segment mix is specific enough that external benchmarks mislead.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, cohort analysis is most valuable for identifying the ACV threshold below which churn is structurally high. This clarifies your ICP more than any segment filter.

options.label: Full retention analytics with cohort analysis, forecasting, and leading indicator correlation
options.insight: You have developed a level of analytics maturity that connects leading indicators with retention outcomes, something many CS teams still aspire to.
options.next_step: Establish a cadence for updating your leading indicator model. Product changes and customer mix shifts alter which signals predict churn, so recalibrate at least annually.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Extend forecasting to account-level renewal probability. Board stakeholders want ARR at risk by account, not averages. Account-level precision is the next frontier.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Connect your leading indicators to your CSM capacity model. If your model flags 40 at-risk accounts next quarter, you need to know now whether you have capacity to act.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, the value of forecasting is in automating the response, not in human review. Route forecast outputs directly to automated intervention triggers.

questions.question: Do you measure time-to-value (TTV)?

options.label: No; we don't track this
options.insight: Without a TTV measure, you have no objective signal of whether customers are reaching the outcome that justified their purchase.
options.next_step: Define your first TTV milestone: the single action or outcome that correlates most strongly with 12-month retention. Track days from contract start to that milestone for the next 10 onboardings.

options.label: Informally; we have a rough sense of how long onboarding takes
options.insight: A rough sense of TTV means you have pattern recognition but no data to improve against or set targets from.
options.next_step: Formalize it: pick one observable, measurable event as your TTV milestone. Log the date for every new customer. You'll have actionable improvement data within one quarter.

options.label: Defined TTV metric with segment-specific targets
options.insight: Segment-specific TTV targets show you understand that different customers have different value timelines, a sign of operational maturity.
options.next_step: Validate your targets: compare stated TTV targets against actual outcomes. If actuals consistently miss by more than 20%, the targets or the onboarding process need revision.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Add an executive stakeholder milestone to TTV. The first time the executive sponsor sees demonstrated ROI (not just technical activation) is the milestone that matters for enterprise retention.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Tie TTV attainment to renewal risk scoring. A cohort that misses TTV by more than 30 days should automatically elevate health risk. Make that connection explicit in your model.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, TTV targets should drive automated check-in triggers, not manual CSM outreach. If a customer hasn't hit the TTV milestone by day 21, an automated sequence should fire.

options.label: TTV tracked, benchmarked, actively optimized, and tied to downstream retention outcomes
options.insight: TTV tied to downstream retention outcomes closes the loop. You can make the business case for onboarding investment in concrete revenue terms.
options.next_step: Quantify the dollar value of each day of TTV improvement using your retention correlation data. Use that figure to make the case for onboarding investment to finance and product teams.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Expand your TTV benchmarks to include multi-stakeholder adoption, not just primary user activation. Enterprise value is realized across teams, so single-user TTV understates time-to-full-value.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Share your TTV benchmarks with your sales team. They're a qualification tool: prospects who miss pre-sale criteria often miss TTV targets too, and that's a solvable problem upstream.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: Benchmark your TTV against self-serve conversion rates. If self-serve customers reach TTV faster, that's evidence to reduce human-touch onboarding and reallocate CSM time.

questions.question: What CS tooling and data infrastructure do you use?

options.label: Spreadsheets and basic CRM only
options.insight: Spreadsheets and CRM alone mean your CS data is fragmented: health signals, customer history, and renewal dates live in separate places.
options.next_step: Clean up your CRM before reaching for new tools or CS platforms. Every account record should have renewal date, ARR, CSM owner, and last contact date. Clean data is the foundation any future platform will rely on.

options.label: CRM with some automation (email sequences, task reminders)
options.insight: CRM automation frees CSMs from manual scheduling work, but the ceiling is the absence of product usage data in your workflow.
options.next_step: Identify what your CRM can't tell you that matters most, likely product usage. Evaluate one integration (product analytics → CRM) before investing in a dedicated CS platform.

options.label: Dedicated CS platform (Gainsight, ChurnZero, Vitally, Totango, etc.)
options.insight: A dedicated CS platform means purpose-built workflows for health scoring, playbook triggers, and customer data in one place.
options.next_step: Audit your platform's adoption within the team. Unused features signal onboarding gaps or workflow misfit; address adoption before adding more configuration.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, ensure your CS platform integrates with CRM and support tooling bidirectionally. Enterprise CS workflows span multiple teams, and siloed platforms create blind spots at handoffs.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Evaluate whether your platform's automation capabilities are being fully utilized. Most mid-market teams configure a platform but run 20% of its potential. Audit playbook activation rates.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, ROI on a full CS platform depends on account volume. Below 200 accounts, a lighter-weight tool with solid usage integration may outperform a full platform on cost-per-insight.

options.label: Integrated CS tech stack with analytics, product telemetry, BI dashboards, and automation
options.insight: An integrated stack with BI and product telemetry means your decisions are based on complete data. Most teams operate with significant instrumentation gaps.
options.next_step: Measure your data latency: how old is the product usage data in your CS platform? If it's more than 48 hours stale, real-time risk detection is compromised. Investigate the pipeline.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure BI dashboards surface account-level data, not just aggregate trends. Board-level CS reporting needs ARR at risk by named account, not portfolio averages.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Use your BI capability to build a capacity model: how many accounts can each CSM handle at what quality level? This drives headcount and territory planning with real data.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, the value of an integrated stack is in automation, not reporting. Redirect BI investment toward decision automation: trigger playbooks from data, not dashboards.

domains.label: Expansion & Value
domains.description: How CS drives expansion revenue, tracks product adoption, and demonstrates value realization to customers.

questions.question: Who owns expansion revenue and how is it managed?

options.label: Sales owns all expansion; CS is not involved
options.insight: CS outside the expansion motion means your account relationship signal (your biggest expansion asset) is not connected to the pipeline.
options.next_step: Ask CSMs to log expansion signals in CRM: contract end date, usage growth, new use case mentions. 15 minutes per account per quarter surfaces pipeline your Sales team can't see.

options.label: CS informally flags opportunities, Sales closes them
options.insight: Informal flagging means expansion is happening, but signal volume and quality are limited by CSM initiative rather than a consistent system.
options.next_step: Define what a qualified expansion signal looks like: specific usage thresholds, adoption milestones, or customer-stated needs. Build a shared CRM field CSMs update monthly.

options.label: CS identifies expansion opportunities with a defined handoff process to Sales or AM
options.insight: A defined handoff turns CS from a passive referrer into a structured pipeline source, a meaningful step toward CS-driven revenue.
options.next_step: Measure your handoff conversion rate: what % of CS-flagged opportunities become closed revenue? If it's below 30%, signal quality or handoff timing needs work.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, add a co-selling motion alongside the handoff. Enterprise expansion deals benefit from joint account planning between CSM and Sales; handoff alone undersells the relationship.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, track average deal size on CS-sourced expansions vs. Sales-sourced ones. If CS flags more strategic deals, that's a case for shifting more ownership to CS.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, automate the handoff trigger in your CS platform. Manual flagging doesn't scale. Fire the signal when usage or adoption crosses a defined threshold.

options.label: CS co-owns or owns expansion with clear metrics, targets, and compensation alignment
options.insight: CS co-ownership of expansion with aligned compensation solves the incentive misalignment that undermines most CS-expansion motions.
options.next_step: Run a quarterly pipeline review for CS-owned expansion: which accounts are expansion-ready, which are stalled, and which stalled deals need a different approach.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, ensure CS expansion targets account for deal size variance. Enterprise expansions are lumpy, and average-based targets can mask a long tail of small-deal activity.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, test whether CS owning expansion vs. handing off to an AM produces different win rates or time-to-close. Use that data to decide where to draw the ownership line.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, CS-owned expansion should be nearly fully automated: usage-triggered upgrade prompts, in-app expansions, automated upsell sequences. Human-touch expansion at SMB is expensive to sustain.

questions.question: How do you track product adoption?

options.label: We don't track adoption in a structured way
options.insight: Without adoption data, your health scoring has a major blind spot: usage is the strongest predictor of renewal intent.
options.next_step: Identify two or three product actions your best customers do consistently that churned customers never did. Instrument those events first; they're your core adoption baseline.

options.label: Basic login or usage data (DAU/MAU, login frequency)
options.insight: Login frequency tells you customers are active but not whether they're getting value, a surface-level adoption signal.
options.next_step: Add one feature-level adoption signal: the feature that most distinguishes retained customers from churned ones in your cohort data. Depth beats breadth at this stage.

options.label: Feature-level adoption tracked by segment with defined adoption milestones
options.insight: Feature-level adoption by segment with defined milestones lets you distinguish growing customers from at-risk ones by what they do, not just how often they log in.
options.next_step: Map adoption milestones to retention outcomes. Which milestones correlate with 90%+ renewal rates? Promote those to leading indicators in your health model.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, extend tracking to multi-team and multi-department usage. Enterprise value is realized when adoption spreads beyond the initial buyer, so single-user depth understates risk.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, use adoption milestones to trigger automatic CSM check-ins. Accounts that reach a milestone are expansion-ready; accounts that stall at a milestone need intervention.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, adoption milestones should drive automated in-app guidance, not CSM outreach. Instrument feature walkthroughs and tooltips that fire when a milestone is missed.

options.label: Adoption tied to customer outcomes, with expansion-readiness signals and automated triggers
options.insight: Adoption tied to outcomes with expansion-readiness triggers lets you identify who's ready to buy more before they ask: a genuine revenue intelligence capability.
options.next_step: Validate expansion-readiness signals against actual expansion revenue. If a signal fires and 70%+ of those accounts expand within 90 days, it's predictive. Below that, recalibrate the threshold.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, build expansion-readiness signals into a formal account growth plan. Enterprise expansion requires a structured conversation, not just a signal-triggered email.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, test whether automated expansion prompts triggered by adoption signals drive as much revenue as CSM-led conversations. Automate what converts at comparable rates.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, expansion-readiness signals should trigger fully automated upgrade flows. If a CSM has to act on the signal, the motion doesn't scale to your account volume.

questions.question: Do you conduct structured business reviews with customers?

options.label: No, or very rarely, only when the customer asks
options.insight: Without structured reviews, there is no recurring moment to demonstrate value at the business level. Renewals are decided without a formal case being made.
options.next_step: Start with one review template for your top 10 accounts: business objectives, progress to date, outcomes delivered, next quarter priorities. Run two or three reviews before scaling the format.

options.label: Annually for top accounts, informal otherwise
options.insight: Annual reviews give you one value-demonstration moment per year, not enough frequency to actively influence the renewal decision.
options.next_step: Move from annual to semi-annual for top accounts and introduce a lighter mid-year check-in for mid-tier. 30 minutes on outcomes is enough; it doesn't need to be a full EBR.

options.label: Quarterly cadence for key accounts with a defined review framework
options.insight: Quarterly reviews with a defined framework give you a structured value conversation four times a year. Most CS teams at this maturity have fewer than two.
options.next_step: Measure QBR completion rate and executive attendance rate separately. A review without the economic buyer present has limited renewal influence, so optimize for executive presence.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, add a forward-looking business case to your QBR format. Retrospective reviews demonstrate past value; outcome projections influence future investment decisions.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, templatize QBR preparation to reduce CSM prep time. If prep takes more than 3 hours per account, the format needs simplification or better tooling.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, quarterly reviews don't scale across your full book. Segment: human QBRs above a defined ARR threshold, automated digital review emails for the rest.

options.label: Regular cadence tailored by segment, outcome-focused with documented action items and follow-through tracking
options.insight: Segment-tailored, outcome-focused reviews with tracked follow-through represent the operational ceiling of business review programs.
options.next_step: Analyze whether review cadence correlates with retention or expansion in your cohort data. If it does, use that finding to make the case for CSM capacity focused on high-quality reviews.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, extend your review program to include a formal roadmap input loop. Customers who influence your product roadmap in reviews have meaningfully higher retention than those who don't.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, track the action items from reviews and their completion rate. Unactioned commitments erode trust; if CS commits in reviews and doesn't follow through, the cadence loses credibility.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, evaluate whether CSM-led reviews at the low end of your portfolio justify the cost. Automate reviews below an ARR threshold and measure whether retention holds.

domains.label: CS Organization & Strategy
domains.description: Organizational positioning of CS, strategic alignment with the business, and team development maturity.

questions.question: Where does Customer Success sit in the organization?

options.label: Unclear or buried deep under another function (Sales, Support, Services)
options.insight: CS buried under another function signals retention is viewed as a cost center. That perception limits your ability to get resources and drive cross-functional change.
options.next_step: Build an internal business case for CS's positioning: document the revenue impact of CS activities on GRR, NRR, and expansion. A clear P&L contribution makes the organizational argument for you.

options.label: Reports to a Sales, Support, or Services leader
options.insight: Reporting into Sales or Support creates structural incentive conflicts. CS success metrics don't naturally align with a Sales leader's priorities or a Support leader's cost focus.
options.next_step: Map the decisions where your reporting structure creates friction: expansion strategy, renewal pricing, QBR format. Document three examples and use them in a conversation about CS independence.

options.label: Reports to CRO, COO, or a dedicated CS VP
options.insight: Reporting to CRO or a dedicated CS VP gives CS strategic visibility and removes the most common conflict-of-interest structures.
options.next_step: With the right reporting line in place, the next risk is CS being defined only by retention. Expand how CS is represented in cross-functional planning: product roadmap, sales enablement, pricing decisions.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure your CS VP has a seat in QBR planning with the executive team. Enterprise CS strategy needs to be set at the same table where enterprise sales strategy is set.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: A CRO reporting line can blur CS and Sales priorities. Ensure CS metrics (GRR, NRR, health) are tracked and reported separately from Sales metrics in leadership reviews.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: CRO reporting often makes CS secondary to new logo growth. Establish your own CS P&L narrative: revenue retained is as valuable as revenue acquired and should be reported alongside it.

options.label: Direct C-suite representation (CCO, or CS VP reporting to CEO) with strategic mandate
options.insight: C-suite CS representation is rare and signals organizational maturity. CS is a strategic function, not a support layer.
options.next_step: With C-suite presence, focus on making CS metrics board-ready: GRR, NRR, at-risk ARR, and CS-influenced expansion should appear in board decks alongside new logo metrics.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure your C-suite CS leader is part of enterprise account strategy conversations, not just retention reviews. Enterprise CS adds value pre-renewal, not only at renewal.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: C-suite CS presence is strongest when CS owns a measurable revenue number. If your CS leader doesn't have a GRR or NRR target, that's the gap to close.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, C-suite CS representation is most valuable when CS is setting the digital and scaled motion strategy. Ensure your CS leader is driving the automation roadmap, not just managing headcount.

questions.question: Is there a documented CS strategy aligned with company goals?

options.label: No; CS operates without a formal strategy
options.insight: Operating without a documented strategy means CS priorities shift with whoever is loudest in the last meeting. There is no forcing function for consistency.
options.next_step: Write a one-page CS strategy: three goals for the year, the metrics that define success for each, and the top two initiatives. Share it with your manager and one cross-functional peer.

options.label: Informal goals exist but aren't documented or aligned cross-functionally
options.insight: Informal goals mean your team has shared intent but no shared accountability. Cross-functional partners don't know what CS is working toward or why.
options.next_step: Document existing goals in one slide or doc. Send it to key cross-functional stakeholders (Sales, Product, Finance) with a request for 30 minutes to align. The act of sharing surfaces the gaps.

options.label: Written CS strategy with defined goals aligned to company OKRs
options.insight: A written strategy aligned to company OKRs means CS is speaking the same language as the rest of the organization, a prerequisite for getting resources and cross-functional support.
options.next_step: Run a quarterly strategy check-in: are your CS OKRs still the right ones given what's changed? Strategy documents that aren't revisited become inaccurate within two quarters.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure your CS strategy explicitly names your 20 most strategic accounts and what CS is doing to protect and grow them. Strategy without account-level specificity stays abstract.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Align your CS strategy to your product roadmap timeline. CS strategy written without knowing what's shipping in the next two quarters creates CSM surprises and customer trust issues.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: Your CS strategy should explicitly define the digital vs. human touch split and at what ACV threshold each applies. Without that, CSMs make individual decisions that create inconsistent customer experiences.

options.label: Comprehensive roadmap with cross-functional alignment, executive sponsorship, and regular strategic reviews
