options.insight: A CS roadmap with executive sponsorship and regular reviews means CS is planned and accountable at the same level as product and sales roadmaps.
options.next_step: Ensure your CS roadmap has a clear dependency view: which initiatives require Product, Sales, or Engineering input? Unmanaged dependencies are where roadmaps slip.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Your CS roadmap should include a named executive sponsorship model for top accounts: who in your company is accountable for each enterprise relationship, beyond the CSM.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Use your strategic review cadence to present a CS P&L: GRR protected, expansion influenced, churn cost avoided. Financial fluency at the leadership level gets CS more resources.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: Your roadmap's biggest lever is automation and tooling investment. Include a clear automation pipeline with expected efficiency gains per initiative. That's how you scale without proportional headcount.

questions.question: How is CS team development and career growth managed?

options.label: No formal development or career path for CS team members
options.insight: Without career paths, retention of your best CSMs is driven by relationship and loyalty, which is fragile, especially as demand for CS talent grows.
options.next_step: Map two CS career tracks informally: IC (CSM → Senior CSM → Principal/Strategic) and management (Team Lead → Manager → Director). Even a draft gives your team a framework for growth conversations.

options.label: Basic onboarding for new hires, informal mentoring
options.insight: Basic onboarding gets CSMs to minimum viable performance, but without development paths, high performers plateau and leave once they outgrow their current role.
options.next_step: Run a skills gap analysis on your current team: list the five skills that define your best-performing CSMs and assess the team against those. That assessment becomes your development roadmap.

options.label: Defined career paths (IC and management tracks) with ongoing training programs
options.insight: Defined career paths with training are a meaningful retention tool. CSMs who can see a path forward are more likely to grow with you than leave for a title bump elsewhere.
options.next_step: Validate that career path criteria are transparent and consistently applied. If CSMs can't describe exactly what they need to do to advance, the paths exist on paper but not in practice.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Add a formal mentorship or shadowing program for enterprise CSMs. Enterprise account complexity requires institutional knowledge transfer; training programs alone don't cover it.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Invest in commercial skills training for CSMs who touch expansion. CSMs who navigate pricing and negotiation conversations close more expansion without needing a Sales overlay every time.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: Career development should include a digital CS track. CSMs who learn to build and optimize automated journeys are your highest-leverage talent as your book of business scales.

options.label: Structured development with certifications, coaching, skills assessment, and internal mobility programs
options.insight: Structured development with internal mobility means your CS function grows its own talent, a competitive advantage in a market where experienced CS talent is expensive to hire.
options.next_step: Measure your internal promotion rate against your attrition rate. If you're promoting 15% and losing 25%, the development pipeline is real but not outpacing attrition. Find where top performers stall.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Expand internal mobility to cross-functional rotations. CSMs spending a quarter in Sales, Product, or Finance develop business fluency that makes them significantly more effective in strategic accounts.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Certifications and structured development are most effective when tied to compensation milestones, not just titles. If development doesn't change pay, high performers disengage from the program.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: Internal mobility programs are most valuable when they feed digital CS and CS Ops tracks: the functions that scale your CS motion without proportional headcount growth.

domains.label: CS Culture & Cross-Functional Alignment
domains.description: How well Customer Success is embedded as a cultural practice across the organization: not just a department, but a shared mindset.

questions.question: How involved is CS in cross-functional discussions (product roadmap, sales handoffs, renewal strategy)?

options.label: CS is rarely invited to cross-functional meetings; other teams make decisions without CS input
options.insight: CS excluded from cross-functional decisions means customer impact data never reaches the people making product, sales, and support choices.
options.next_step: Pick one meeting per function where CS should have a standing seat: product planning, sales pipeline review, support escalation. Request an invite and come prepared with 2-3 customer data points.

options.label: CS is occasionally consulted on customer-related decisions but isn't a regular participant
options.insight: Occasional consultation means CS input is sought reactively, after the decision frame is already set, rather than shaping it from the start.
options.next_step: Ask each function which meeting they'd most benefit from CS input in. Then attend consistently enough that your absence is noticed when you can't make it.

options.label: CS has a standing seat at key cross-functional meetings (product, sales, support) and contributes to planning
options.insight: A standing seat in cross-functional planning means CS is shaping decisions before they reach customers, not just responding afterward.
options.next_step: Audit the quality of CS's contribution: are you bringing specific customer data and named examples, or general observations? Specific data influences decisions; general observations don't.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, expand CS's involvement to individual account strategy sessions. Enterprise deals are complex enough that account-level CS input belongs in sales and renewal planning, not just category-level.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: In mid-market, use your cross-functional presence to drive a standardized sales-to-CS handoff. If Sales is making commitments CS can't honor, that's a planning conversation CS must be in to prevent it.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, CS's most valuable cross-functional contribution is volume data: patterns across hundreds of accounts. Bring that data to every meeting, not individual account stories.

options.label: CS is deeply integrated into all customer-impacting decisions; other teams proactively seek CS perspective
options.insight: Other teams proactively seeking CS perspective means customer context is part of how your organization thinks, not just what CS reports.
options.next_step: Sustain this by systematizing how CS input flows: document which customer signals go to which teams, at what cadence, and in what format. Proactive sharing shouldn't depend on who's in the room.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Extend CS integration to include the board narrative. Enterprise CS data (logo retention, strategic account health, expansion pipeline) should appear in investor and board updates.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Ensure CS's cross-functional integration is bidirectional: CS gets product usage, support sentiment, and billing signals back, not just contributes customer voice outward.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, sustaining CS integration means building automated signal sharing: customer health dashboards visible to Sales and Product, not just CS. Remove the dependency on CS manually broadcasting.

questions.question: How well do other teams (Sales, Product, Engineering, Support) understand what CS does and why it matters?

options.label: Other teams see CS as reactive support or glorified account management with unclear value
options.insight: Being seen as reactive support means CS is measured against the wrong benchmark, and any initiative to expand CS's scope will meet resistance.
options.next_step: Run one internal CS value session with a single team (Sales or Product), showing three specific examples of CS actions that protected or grew revenue. One concrete example shifts perception faster than a general narrative.

options.label: Some teams understand CS's role, but there's frequent confusion about responsibilities and boundaries
options.insight: Confusion about CS boundaries costs time in every cross-functional interaction. Unclear ownership creates escalation loops and duplicated effort.
options.next_step: Write a one-page CS charter: what CS owns, what it doesn't, and how to work with CS. Share it with Sales, Product, and Support leads. A shared definition prevents most boundary disputes.

options.label: Most teams understand CS's strategic value and actively collaborate on customer outcomes
options.insight: Active collaboration on customer outcomes means CS's value is recognized. The next challenge is making that collaboration scalable and consistent.
options.next_step: Document the collaboration that's working: which teams, which cadence, what format. Export that model to teams where collaboration is weaker. What works with Product may work with Engineering.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure Sales and CS have a shared account-level view of each strategic customer. Collaboration breaks down when teams are working from different pictures of the same account.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Formalize CS's role in the sales-to-CS handoff as a structured event, not an email thread. A 30-minute handoff call with a standard template prevents most post-sale CS surprises.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, active collaboration needs to be lightweight. Define the minimum viable touchpoint for each function (the one thing each team does that requires CS alignment) and protect only those.

options.label: CS's mission and impact are universally understood; customer success is seen as everyone's responsibility
options.insight: Customer success as a shared organizational value, not just a department, is the cultural outcome most CS leaders aspire to and very few achieve.
options.next_step: Protect this culture as the organization scales. New hires default to departmental thinking. Build CS's mission into onboarding for Sales, Product, and Engineering, not just for CS new hires.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Embed the shared-value culture into your account escalation protocols. When a strategic account is at risk, the cross-functional response should be automatic, not dependent on CS escalating to each team manually.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Codify shared ownership in OKRs: give Product and Sales a metric they own jointly with CS (NRR, feature adoption rate). Shared metrics create shared accountability more effectively than shared values alone.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, shared CS culture is most sustainable when it's data-visible. Give all teams access to the customer health dashboard. Visibility to outcomes changes behavior better than communication campaigns.

questions.question: To what extent are customer outcomes (retention, expansion, health) treated as a shared responsibility across teams?

options.label: Customer retention is solely CS's problem; other teams focus only on their own metrics
options.insight: Retention owned only by CS means churn is treated as a CS execution failure, even when the causes trace to product, sales promises, or support quality.
options.next_step: Do a churn root-cause analysis on your last 10 lost accounts. Categorize the primary reason by owning team. If 30%+ of churn traces to product gaps or bad sales promises, that's a cross-functional conversation to start.

options.label: There's lip service to shared ownership, but CS still bears most of the accountability for churn and renewals
options.insight: Nominal shared ownership without metric accountability means CS is the only team measured on outcomes it can't fully control.
options.next_step: Propose adding a retention metric to one other team's OKRs (Sales' expansion bookings or Product's feature adoption rate) with CS data as the measurement source. One shared metric changes the conversation.

options.label: Key customer metrics (NRR, churn, health) are visible to and partially owned by multiple teams
options.insight: Multi-team visibility to NRR and churn changes the conversation from 'CS's problem' to 'our signal', a meaningful cultural shift.
options.next_step: Move from shared visibility to shared accountability. Pick one customer metric and give another team co-ownership of it. Expansion NRR shared with Sales is a natural place to start.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Extend metric visibility to account-level health dashboards accessible to Sales, Support, and Product leadership. Account-level data creates account-level accountability.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Tie shared metric visibility to the product roadmap process. If Product can see that a feature correlates with 20% lower churn, it becomes a prioritization input, not just a CS data point.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, shared metric visibility is most powerful when it's automated. A weekly NRR and health summary sent to Sales and Product leadership creates consistent awareness without a CS broadcast dependency.

options.label: Customer outcomes are a company-wide OKR; Sales, Product, and CS share accountability with aligned incentives
options.insight: Company-wide OKR alignment with incentive alignment across teams is the structural foundation of a genuinely customer-centric organization.
options.next_step: Audit whether incentives actually align in practice: do Sales comp plans penalize churn they cause, or only reward bookings? Structural alignment is only real when it survives the comp plan review.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure strategic account health appears in board and investor reporting alongside ARR. Shared ownership becomes durable when it's externally visible and measured at the highest level.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Run a quarterly cross-functional review of your top 20 accounts (health, risk, and expansion opportunity) with Sales, CS, and Product in the room. Shared review creates shared ownership better than shared dashboards.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, company-wide OKR alignment is most effective when automated: trigger cross-functional alerts when cohort health drops below a threshold. Remove the dependency on CS manually escalating to other teams.

domains.label: AI Leverage in CS
domains.description: How CS teams apply AI to augment CSMs, predict risk, and orchestrate interventions, and whether they do so responsibly and at scale.

questions.question: Which best describes the AI capabilities actively used in your CS workflows today?

options.label: No AI beyond generic productivity tools like email autocomplete or spellcheck
options.insight: Relying only on generic productivity AI means you're not yet capturing the CS-specific value in drafting, summarizing, and pattern-recognition workflows.
options.next_step: Start with one CS-specific AI use case: call summary automation or email drafting with customer context. Pick the task CSMs do most repetitively and pilot one tool for 30 days before evaluating impact.

options.label: CSMs use drafting and summarization copilots for emails, call notes, ticket summaries, and meeting prep
options.insight: AI drafting and summarization in active use means CSMs are recovering time from administrative tasks, the foundation for moving to higher-value AI applications.
options.next_step: Measure the time saved per CSM per week from current AI use. If it's less than 2 hours, adoption or prompt quality is the bottleneck; the tools are likely sufficient.

options.label: Predictive models (health scoring, churn risk, or expansion propensity) are in production and integrated into CSM workflows
options.insight: Predictive models are running inside your CSM workflows. That puts you ahead of teams still using AI only for drafting and summaries.
options.next_step: Validate model precision and recall against actual outcomes quarterly. A model that fires 40% accurate churn alerts creates alert fatigue and erodes CSM trust faster than no model at all.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure predictive models include relationship signals (exec sponsor engagement, stakeholder network breadth) alongside product usage. Enterprise churn often starts in the relationship, not in the data.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Prioritize churn risk prediction over expansion propensity at mid-market ACV. One prevented churn is worth more than one expansion identified at this scale, so sequence your AI investments accordingly.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, predictive models are valuable only when they trigger automated responses, not human ones. If a churn risk flag requires CSM action, the model can't scale across hundreds of accounts.

options.label: Agentic AI takes bounded autonomous actions (tiered interventions, routing, self-serve resolution) with explicit human oversight and measured outcomes
options.insight: Agentic AI with bounded autonomy and measured outcomes is the frontier of CS AI maturity. Very few teams are operating here with real governance in place.
options.next_step: Establish a failure mode review for autonomous AI actions. When an agent produces a bad outcome, what's the recovery path and how is the model updated? That review process is what makes autonomy safe.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: For enterprise, keep human oversight requirements higher than your default. Enterprise customers often have relationship expectations that autonomous AI interactions, even well-executed ones, can violate.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Use A/B testing to compare AI-assisted vs. unassisted interventions on matched account cohorts. That test data is what lets you confidently expand AI autonomy over time.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, agentic AI is most defensible when applied to fully self-serve account tiers. Reserve higher-autonomy AI for accounts below a defined ACV threshold where human-touch cost outweighs recovery value.

questions.question: How well does your data foundation support AI in CS?

options.label: Customer data lives in silos across CRM, support, product analytics, and billing; no unified view exists
options.insight: Siloed customer data means any AI model you build is partial. It can only see part of the customer, which limits what it can predict or act on.
options.next_step: Map your data silos: list every system that holds customer data and the fields most valuable in a unified view. That map is the starting point for any data platform conversation with Engineering or BI.

options.label: Core CS data is consolidated in a CS platform or data warehouse, but not feature-engineered for AI; most pulls are manual
options.insight: Consolidated but unengineered data means you have the ingredients but not the preparation. AI models require structured, clean, and feature-ready inputs to produce reliable outputs.
options.next_step: Identify the 10 fields an AI health scoring model would need and check whether they're populated, accurate, and consistently formatted. Data quality on 10 fields is more valuable than breadth across 100.

options.label: A unified customer data layer feeds purpose-built features to AI models; product, support, sentiment, and billing signals flow in, and outputs flow back to CSM tools
options.insight: A unified data layer with bidirectional flow (from systems to model, and from model outputs back to CSM tools) is the infrastructure foundation that makes AI in CS production-grade.
options.next_step: Measure data freshness: how old is the product usage data feeding your models? If it's more than 48 hours stale, your risk models are reacting to yesterday's signals.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure your data layer includes relationship signals: executive engagement, org chart changes, contract amendment history. Structured product data alone misses the relationship dimension of enterprise churn.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Use your unified data layer to build a consistent feature set across accounts: the same fields for every account. Inconsistency in feature coverage degrades model accuracy across a large portion of your book.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, your data layer's most important job is enabling automated interventions without CSM input. Verify that model outputs are routed to action systems, not just dashboards.

options.label: Real-time multi-signal integration with continuous model retraining and feedback loops from CSM actions back into the models
options.insight: Real-time integration with continuous retraining and CSM feedback loops is an advanced ML operations capability that most enterprise software companies haven't built specifically for CS.
options.next_step: Audit your feedback loop quality: are CSM actions (overrides, interventions, outcomes) actually flowing back into model training, or just into logs? If only logs, the feedback loop is broken.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure your real-time data layer has explicit handling for high-stakes accounts: flags that prevent automated low-confidence actions on your most strategic logos.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Use continuous retraining to adapt to seasonal patterns in your customer base. Mid-market retention often has quarterly dynamics; models trained on annual data will miss them.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, continuous retraining is most valuable for churn prediction. SMB churn tends to have faster-moving signals than enterprise, so retrain at least quarterly, not annually.

questions.question: How mature is your governance, adoption, and measurement of AI in CS?

options.label: Ad hoc use with no policy on AI tools, PII handling, or hallucination review; adoption varies by individual CSM
options.insight: Ad hoc AI use without policy means exposure to PII mishandling, inconsistent customer experience, and no way to measure or improve what AI is contributing.
options.next_step: Write a one-page AI use policy for CS: which tools are approved, which customer data can be used as input, and what review is required before AI-drafted content is sent to a customer. Start simple.

options.label: Written guidelines cover PII, prompt hygiene, and human-in-loop requirements; some CSMs use AI regularly, but adoption is uneven
options.insight: Guidelines in place with uneven adoption means the policy exists but hasn't become practice, leaving your risk exposure nearly as high as without a policy.
options.next_step: Identify the CSMs with the highest AI adoption and make them internal champions. Peer demonstrations and shared prompt templates are more effective at closing adoption gaps than policy reminders.

options.label: Formal Responsible AI principles, CSM training, majority daily use, and measured impact on time saved, accuracy, and outcomes
options.insight: Formal principles with measured impact and majority adoption means AI in CS is a managed capability, not an experiment: a significant operational advantage.
options.next_step: Add outcome metrics alongside efficiency metrics: not just 'time saved' but 'response rate change' and 'health score accuracy.' Efficiency gains justify cost; outcome gains justify strategy.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Ensure your Responsible AI principles explicitly cover customer disclosure. Enterprise customers sometimes need to know when they're interacting with AI-assisted communication rather than a CSM directly.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Use your measured adoption data to identify the AI use cases with the highest CSM satisfaction and the highest outcome impact. Double down on those before adding new capabilities.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, majority AI adoption is most valuable when it enables scale, not just quality. Measure adoption in terms of accounts-per-CSM capacity increase, not just hours saved per CSM.

options.label: AI impact is a tracked KPI tied to retention, expansion, and CSAT; model reviews, A/B tests of assisted vs. unassisted playbooks, and a governance committee are routine
options.insight: AI impact tracked as a business KPI with A/B testing and a governance committee means your CS AI program is run like a product: sustained, measured, and improved over time.
options.next_step: Ensure your governance committee includes a customer perspective, through advisory input or analysis of customer response patterns to AI-assisted interactions. Governance without the customer view optimizes for efficiency, not relationship quality.

next_step_by_segment.seg_enterprise: Publish your AI governance standards to key customers who ask. Transparency about how AI is used in enterprise accounts builds trust and differentiates you from vendors who don't have a clear answer.
next_step_by_segment.seg_midmarket: Use your A/B test results to build a portfolio view of AI investments: which capabilities produce ROI vs. which are marginal. Retire marginal ones to fund the next generation.
next_step_by_segment.seg_smb: At SMB scale, the governance committee's most important role is approving new autonomy tiers. Define what class of AI action requires committee review vs. what CS Ops can deploy directly.

metrics_directory.description: Catalog of CS metrics with contextual recommendations by maturity stage and company profile.

metrics.name: Net Revenue Retention (NRR)
metrics.definition: The percentage of recurring revenue retained from existing customers over a period, including expansion, contraction, and churn. NRR above 100% means the customer base is growing without new logos.
metrics.formula: (Starting ARR + Expansion - Contraction - Churn) / Starting ARR × 100

why_priority.crawl: Focus on GRR first. You need to stop the bleeding before optimizing for growth. NRR is noise when your churn fundamentals aren't solid.
why_priority.walk: Start tracking NRR alongside GRR. Use it to build the case for CS as a revenue function, but don't optimize for it yet.
why_priority.run: NRR should be a primary CS metric. Your expansion motions and risk management should directly influence this number.

common_mistakes: Tracking NRR before you have reliable GRR. You can't grow what you can't retain
Blending all segments into one NRR number. Segment-level NRR reveals where the real problems and opportunities are
Confusing NRR with logo retention. You can have great NRR with terrible logo churn if a few large accounts expand

audience.relevance: Report monthly to leadership as the definitive measure of CS’s impact on revenue growth.
Board-level metric showing whether the installed base is growing or shrinking without new logos.
Track by team and segment to identify which CSMs and accounts are driving net growth.

metrics.name: Gross Revenue Retention (GRR)
metrics.definition: The percentage of recurring revenue retained from existing customers, excluding expansion. GRR can never exceed 100%. It purely measures your ability to keep what you have.
metrics.formula: (Starting ARR - Contraction - Churn) / Starting ARR × 100

why_priority.crawl: This is your most important metric. If GRR is low, nothing else matters; you're losing the foundation.
why_priority.walk: GRR remains critical. Your playbooks and health scores should be directly aimed at improving this number.
why_priority.run: GRR should be stable and high. Now focus on understanding GRR by cohort and segment to find hidden weaknesses.

common_mistakes: Ignoring GRR because NRR looks healthy. Expansion can mask a retention problem for a while, but not forever
Not separating voluntary churn (customer chose to leave) from involuntary churn (payment failure, company closed)
Measuring GRR annually only. Monthly or quarterly cohort GRR catches trends much earlier

audience.relevance: Monitor weekly to catch emerging retention issues before they become trends.
Report monthly to leadership as the baseline health indicator of the customer base.
Understand GRR trends in your book of business to prioritize at-risk accounts.
GRR is the foundation of sustainable growth. If it’s declining, scaling efforts are wasted.

metrics.name: Logo (Customer) Retention Rate
metrics.definition: The percentage of customers retained over a period, ignoring contract size. Every account counts equally, so a churned $5K SMB and a churned $500K enterprise both register as one lost logo.
metrics.formula: (Customers at End - New Customers Added) / Customers at Start × 100

why_priority.crawl: Track this alongside GRR. It's the simplest retention metric and tells you the raw reality of how many customers are leaving.
why_priority.walk: Segment logo retention by tier and cohort. Patterns here drive your risk management playbooks.
why_priority.run: Logo retention should be stable. Focus on understanding which segments and cohorts deviate and why.

common_mistakes: Treating all logo churn equally. Losing a $500K enterprise account is very different from losing a $5K SMB account
Not tracking first-year vs. mature customer churn separately. First-year churn signals onboarding and sales alignment issues
Reviewing the number annually only. Monthly tracking surfaces problems while there is still time to intervene
Counting heavy downgrades as retention. A customer that cuts seats by 80% is functionally close to churned, even though the logo is still on the books

audience.relevance: Track monthly to understand customer attrition in your portfolio and identify patterns.
Review by cohort and segment to spot systemic retention problems across the team.
Use alongside GRR to separate 'how many customers leave' from 'how much revenue leaves.'

metrics.name: Time to Value (TTV)
metrics.definition: The time it takes for a new customer to achieve their first meaningful outcome or value milestone after purchase. This is not just 'go live'; it's when the customer experiences the value they were promised.
metrics.formula: Date of first value milestone - Date of contract start (in days)

why_priority.crawl: Start by defining what 'value' means for your product. Even an informal TTV target is better than nothing.
why_priority.walk: Define TTV formally by segment. This becomes the north star for your onboarding playbook.
why_priority.run: TTV should be tracked, benchmarked, and actively optimized. Correlate TTV with first-year retention to prove its impact.

common_mistakes: Defining TTV as 'go live' rather than 'first value realized'. A customer can be live and still not getting value
Having one TTV target across all segments. Enterprise onboarding is fundamentally different from SMB self-serve
Not connecting TTV to downstream metrics like retention. If you can't prove faster TTV leads to better retention, it's just a vanity metric

audience.relevance: Track for each new customer to ensure they hit value milestones on time and flag delays early.
Benchmark across the team and identify which CSMs or segments consistently achieve faster TTV.
Use TTV data to identify product friction that slows time-to-value and prioritize UX improvements.

metrics.name: Customer Health Score
metrics.definition: A composite score that evaluates the overall health of a customer relationship, typically combining product usage, engagement quality, support sentiment, and business outcomes into a single indicator.
metrics.formula: Varies. Typically a weighted combination of: Product usage signals (30-40%) + Engagement frequency and quality (20-30%) + Support ticket sentiment and volume (15-20%) + Business outcome achievement (15-20%)

why_priority.crawl: Start simple. Even a 3-color RAG status (red/amber/green) based on 3 signals is better than nothing. Don't over-engineer it.
why_priority.walk: Build a real health score with 5–7 weighted inputs. The key is consistency: every CSM should assess health the same way.
why_priority.run: Health scores should be automated and trigger workflows. Focus on predictive accuracy: does your score actually predict churn?

crawl.scoring_method: Simple RAG (Red/Amber/Green) updated monthly by CSM
crawl.what_to_skip: Don't try to build a 10-factor weighted model. It will be inaccurate and nobody will maintain it.

recommended_inputs: Product usage depth (feature adoption, not just logins)
walk.scoring_method: Weighted score (0–100) with defined thresholds for healthy/at-risk/critical
walk.what_to_skip: Don't add NPS as a health input yet unless you have reliable, frequent NPS data. Annual NPS is too lagging.

recommended_inputs: Product usage trends (not just current state)
Business outcome achievement vs. success plan
run.scoring_method: Automated composite score with trend indicators and automated alerts at threshold crossings
run.what_to_skip: Don't rely solely on automated scores. Keep a CSM override mechanism for qualitative signals the data can't capture.

common_mistakes: Building a complex health score before you have reliable data inputs: garbage in, garbage out
Making health scores entirely subjective (CSM opinion) or entirely objective (data only). The best scores blend both
Not validating the score against actual outcomes. If red accounts aren't churning more than green accounts, your score is broken
Updating health scores too infrequently. Monthly is minimum; weekly is better for high-touch accounts

audience.relevance: Check daily to identify at-risk accounts and prioritize outreach to declining health scores.
Review weekly to spot portfolio-level health trends and allocate resources to struggling segments.
Track aggregate health distribution as a leading indicator of future retention and expansion.
Correlate health score components with product usage to identify features that drive customer success.

metrics.name: Expansion Revenue
metrics.definition: Additional recurring revenue from existing customers, generated through upsells, cross-sells, seat expansion, or plan upgrades. Reported as new ARR closed in-period, separate from renewals and net of any contraction.
metrics.formula: Total new ARR from existing customers in period

why_priority.crawl: Don't focus here yet. Retain first, expand later. Premature expansion focus can damage customer trust.
why_priority.walk: Start tracking expansion informally. Identify which customers expanded and why, then look for patterns.
why_priority.run: Expansion should be a formal CS metric with defined processes for identifying and advancing opportunities.

common_mistakes: Pushing expansion before the customer has achieved first value. This erodes trust fast
Not distinguishing organic expansion (customer naturally grows) from CS-driven expansion (proactive opportunity identification)
Crediting expansion entirely to Sales or entirely to CS. Misalignment here creates organizational friction
Treating every seat add as expansion. Contractual true-ups and natural growth on usage-based plans are different from CS-influenced expansion, and mixing them flatters the metric

audience.relevance: Identify expansion-ready accounts in your book and flag opportunities for upsell conversations.
Track expansion pipeline and conversion rates across the team to forecast growth from existing accounts.
Monitor as a key indicator of CS’s revenue contribution and alignment with sales-led growth.

metrics.name: Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT)
metrics.definition: A measure of customer satisfaction with a specific interaction or experience, typically scored on a 1–5 or 1–7 scale. Best read at the touchpoint level (post-onboarding, post-support), not as a single account-wide number.
metrics.formula: (Number of satisfied responses [4-5 on a 5-point scale] / Total responses) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Useful as a quick pulse check, especially post-onboarding. Easy to implement.
why_priority.walk: Track CSAT at key lifecycle touchpoints. Use it as one input to health scores.
why_priority.run: CSAT is useful but limited. Supplement with CES and outcome-based metrics.

common_mistakes: Using CSAT as your primary CS metric. Satisfaction doesn't predict retention as strongly as you'd think
Survey fatigue: too many CSAT surveys reduce response rates and data quality
Not acting on low CSAT scores. Collecting data without a response loop makes customers feel ignored
Conflating CSAT with NPS or CES. Each measures something different (satisfaction with an event, loyalty, perceived effort), and mixing them in one dashboard hides the signal each is best at

audience.relevance: Review after key interactions (onboarding, QBRs) to gauge satisfaction and course-correct quickly.
Aggregate across the team to identify CSMs or segments with consistently low satisfaction.
Use CSAT trends to prioritize product improvements that directly impact customer experience.

metrics.name: Product Adoption Rate
metrics.definition: The degree to which customers are actively using key features and capabilities of your product relative to what's available and relevant to their use case.
metrics.formula: Varies by context. Common: (Active users of feature / Total licensed users) × 100, or (Features actively used / Features available in plan) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Start with the basics: are customers logging in? How often? Even crude usage data is valuable.
why_priority.walk: Move beyond logins to feature-level adoption. Define what 'healthy adoption' looks like for each segment.
why_priority.run: Adoption should be a leading indicator in your health score. Correlate adoption patterns with retention outcomes.

common_mistakes: Tracking logins as adoption. Logging in is not the same as deriving value
Using one adoption metric across all segments. Enterprise adoption looks very different from SMB
Not defining 'sticky features': the features that, when adopted, most strongly predict retention

audience.relevance: Monitor per-account adoption to identify customers who aren’t using key features and intervene.
Track adoption trends across segments to inform training priorities and playbook updates.
Identify low-adoption features to determine if they need better UX, documentation, or deprecation.
Correlate adoption rates with retention to prove the business case for adoption-focused CS motions.

metrics.name: Time to First Value (TTFV)
metrics.definition: The number of days from contract signing to the customer achieving their first defined success milestone. Unlike Time to Value which may include full go-live, TTFV focuses on the earliest meaningful moment of value realization.
metrics.formula: Date of first success milestone achieved - Contract start date (in days)

why_priority.crawl: Define what 'first value' means for your product. Even a rough milestone is better than no milestone.
why_priority.walk: Track TTFV by segment and optimize your onboarding to hit it faster. Faster TTFV = better first-year retention.
why_priority.run: TTFV should be a leading KPI with segment-specific targets. Automate milestone tracking and alert on delays.

common_mistakes: Confusing TTFV with go-live date. Going live doesn’t mean value was achieved
Using the same TTFV target across all segments. Enterprise vs. SMB have wildly different timelines
Not connecting TTFV to downstream retention metrics to prove ROI of onboarding improvements
Measuring TTFV without defining clear, customer-validated success milestones

audience.relevance: Track for each new customer to ensure first value milestones are hit within target timelines.
Benchmark TTFV across the team and identify bottlenecks in the onboarding process.
Report as a leading indicator: faster TTFV strongly correlates with better first-year retention.

metrics.name: Onboarding Completion Rate
metrics.definition: The percentage of new customers who complete all defined onboarding steps within the target onboarding window. Measures the effectiveness of your onboarding program at driving customers through to readiness.
metrics.formula: (Customers completing all onboarding steps within target window / Total new customers in cohort) × 100

why_priority.crawl: First, define what 'complete onboarding' means. Document your onboarding steps before you can measure completion.
why_priority.walk: Track completion rate and identify where customers get stuck. This is the foundation for onboarding optimization.
why_priority.run: Segment-specific completion targets should exist. Correlate completion rate with first-year retention to prove impact.

common_mistakes: Not defining onboarding steps clearly enough to measure completion objectively
Setting the onboarding window too short or too long for the product complexity
Counting partial completion as success. A customer who does 4 of 5 steps may still not get value
Not following up with customers who stall partway through onboarding

audience.relevance: Monitor each customer’s onboarding progress and proactively intervene when steps are incomplete.
Identify which onboarding steps have the highest drop-off rates and redesign them.
Use completion data to simplify onboarding flows and reduce product-side friction.

metrics.name: Product Adoption Depth
metrics.definition: The percentage of licensed features, modules, or capabilities that a customer is actively using. Unlike basic adoption rate which may just track logins, adoption depth measures how deeply customers engage with the full product surface.
metrics.formula: (Features/modules actively used / Total features/modules available in customer’s plan) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Track basic product usage first (logins, active users). Feature-level tracking can wait.
why_priority.walk: Define what 'active use' means for each key feature. Start tracking adoption depth for top-tier accounts.
why_priority.run: Adoption depth should be a health score input and expansion indicator. Low depth = at-risk; high depth = expansion-ready.

common_mistakes: Treating all features equally. Some features are core, others are nice-to-have
Not adjusting expectations by plan tier. A customer on a basic plan shouldn’t be measured against enterprise features
Measuring adoption without context of use case. A customer may not need every feature to get value
Ignoring adoption velocity. How quickly customers adopt features matters as much as total adoption

audience.relevance: Identify underutilized features in accounts and create enablement plans to deepen adoption.
Track adoption depth trends across the portfolio to inform training and enablement strategy.
Understand which features customers adopt and which they ignore to inform product roadmap.
Use adoption depth as an expansion readiness signal: deeply adopted customers are prime upsell candidates.

metrics.name: DAU/MAU Ratio (Stickiness)
metrics.definition: The ratio of daily active users to monthly active users, indicating how habitually customers use the product. A higher ratio means more users return daily, signaling strong product-market fit and engagement.
metrics.formula: Daily Active Users (DAU) / Monthly Active Users (MAU) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Track basic MAU first. DAU/MAU ratio requires reliable daily usage data you may not have yet.
why_priority.walk: Start tracking DAU/MAU for your core product. Use it as a leading indicator alongside health scores.
why_priority.run: DAU/MAU should be tracked by segment and product area. Use it to identify products/features with strongest stickiness.

common_mistakes: Expecting enterprise B2B products to match consumer app DAU/MAU ratios. B2B products naturally have lower daily usage
Not accounting for product type. A weekly reporting tool will naturally have lower daily engagement
Counting automated or API activity as 'active use'. Measure human engagement separately
Obsessing over DAU/MAU when your product is inherently not a daily-use tool

audience.relevance: Use DAU/MAU to measure product-market fit and identify the most engaging product areas.
Track stickiness by segment to identify which customer types find the most daily value.
Monitor as a leading indicator of retention: sticky products retain better.

metrics.name: Expansion Revenue Rate
metrics.definition: The percentage of total recurring revenue from existing customers that comes from upsells, cross-sells, and seat expansion over a given period. Measures how effectively the organization grows within its installed base.
metrics.formula: (Expansion ARR in period / Starting ARR of existing customers) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Don’t focus on expansion yet. Retain first; pushing expansion before customers have value damages trust.
why_priority.walk: Begin tracking which accounts expanded and why. Look for patterns but don’t formalize expansion targets yet.
why_priority.run: Expansion rate should be a formal metric. CS should have defined processes for identifying and qualifying expansion opportunities.

common_mistakes: Conflating organic growth (customer naturally adds users) with proactive expansion (CS-driven upsell)
Not tracking expansion by source. Was it CS-identified, Sales-identified, or product-led?
Setting expansion targets before retention is stable. Expansion on a churning base is a losing strategy
Ignoring contraction alongside expansion. Net expansion (expansion minus downgrades) is more meaningful

audience.relevance: Track expansion rate by CSM and segment to identify top performers and replicable motions.
Report to leadership as a measure of CS’s contribution to revenue growth from the installed base.
Use to forecast future revenue from existing customers and balance new vs. expansion investment.

metrics.name: CAC Payback Period
metrics.definition: The number of months required to recover the cost of acquiring a customer through their recurring revenue contribution. A shorter payback period means faster return on acquisition investment.
metrics.formula: Customer Acquisition Cost / (Monthly Recurring Revenue × Gross Margin %)

why_priority.crawl: Not a CS metric at this stage. Focus on retention fundamentals before worrying about unit economics.
why_priority.walk: Awareness only. Understanding CAC payback helps CS leaders make the case for investment in customer retention.
why_priority.run: Track CAC payback by segment. CS’s impact on retention directly affects payback: shorter churn means faster payback.

common_mistakes: Not including all acquisition costs (marketing, sales, onboarding) in the CAC calculation
Ignoring gross margin. Revenue ≠ profit, and payback should reflect actual contribution
Not segmenting CAC payback. Blended payback hides massive segment-level differences
Treating CAC payback as purely a finance/sales metric when CS retention directly impacts it

audience.relevance: Use to justify CS investment: better retention directly shortens payback period.
Critical for unit economics and investor reporting: shows sustainability of the growth model.
Fundamental health metric: long payback periods signal a business model problem.

metrics.name: Customer Effort Score (CES)
metrics.definition: A measure of how easy customers find it to accomplish their goals with your product and team. Lower effort correlates with higher loyalty. Typically measured on a 1-7 scale where 1 = very difficult and 7 = very easy.
metrics.formula: (Sum of all effort ratings / Number of responses), on a 1-7 scale

why_priority.crawl: Not a priority yet. CSAT is easier to implement and gives you a basic sentiment baseline.
why_priority.walk: Consider adding CES to post-interaction surveys (after support tickets, after onboarding). It’s more actionable than CSAT.
why_priority.run: CES should supplement CSAT as a health score input. High-effort experiences are strong churn predictors.

common_mistakes: Only measuring CES after support interactions. Measure it across the full journey (onboarding, upgrades, renewals)
Not acting on CES data. Collecting effort scores without reducing friction is worse than not measuring at all
Using CES as a standalone metric. It’s most powerful when combined with CSAT and outcome data
Survey fatigue: asking CES too frequently reduces response rates and data quality

audience.relevance: Review after key touchpoints to identify high-effort experiences and address them proactively.
Analyze effort hotspots across the customer journey to prioritize process improvements.
Use CES data to reduce product friction: high-effort interactions often point to UX problems.

metrics.name: Escalation Rate
metrics.definition: The percentage of accounts that require escalation to management or executive involvement within a given period. High escalation rates may indicate systemic product issues, misaligned expectations, or CSM capability gaps.
metrics.formula: (Number of accounts with escalations in period / Total active accounts) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Track escalations informally. At this stage, every escalation is a learning opportunity about your product and processes.
why_priority.walk: Define what constitutes an escalation vs. a normal support issue. Start categorizing escalation types and root causes.
why_priority.run: Escalation rate should be tracked formally with trend analysis. Use root cause data to drive product and process improvements.

common_mistakes: Not distinguishing between proactive escalations (CSM caught risk early) and reactive escalations (customer complained to exec)
Penalizing CSMs for escalating. This discourages early risk detection and makes the problem worse
Not tracking escalation resolution rate and time alongside escalation rate
Ignoring repeat escalations from the same account. This signals a deeper, unresolved issue

audience.relevance: Track monthly to identify CSMs or accounts with high escalation frequency and intervene.
Monitor trends to detect systemic issues: rising escalation rates signal product or process failures.
Use to identify recurring escalation triggers in your accounts and build proactive mitigation plans.

metrics.name: AI-Assisted Resolution Rate
metrics.definition: The percentage of CS-initiated interventions in a period where AI played a material role in the outcome, including drafting the response, surfacing the risk signal, generating the recommendation, or taking a bounded autonomous action. Measures how much of CS delivery is being augmented or automated by AI.
metrics.formula: (CS interventions with documented AI involvement / Total CS interventions in period) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Don't measure this yet. You're still deciding whether to use AI tools at all, and tracking a metric for a capability you haven't adopted creates noise.
why_priority.walk: Start tracking informally once you have a pilot (call summarization, health scoring copilot). Establishes a baseline before scaling.
why_priority.run: Track formally. At this stage AI is embedded in multiple workflows, so you need to know the coverage and whether AI-assisted interventions produce better retention outcomes.

common_mistakes: Counting AI tool usage (e.g., CSM opened the copilot) instead of AI-materially-influenced outcomes. Activity is not impact
Including interventions where AI was used but the output was completely rewritten. Set a minimum threshold for 'material involvement'
Optimizing this metric without measuring outcome quality. A high rate with declining CSAT or GRR means AI is creating noise, not value
Not distinguishing between AI-drafted content, AI-surfaced signals, and autonomous AI actions. These have very different reliability profiles

audience.relevance: Use to measure AI program ROI and set targets for expanding AI coverage across CS workflows.
Track and report monthly. Own the definition of 'material AI involvement' and ensure consistent tagging across workflows.
Compare AI-assisted vs. unassisted outcomes by CSM and account segment to identify where AI adds the most value.

metrics.name: CSM AI Adoption Rate
metrics.definition: The percentage of CSMs actively using approved AI tools in their weekly workflow. Measures team-wide AI adoption as a leading indicator of potential AI-driven productivity and outcome gains. 'Active use' means at least three documented AI-assisted interactions per week.
metrics.formula: (CSMs with 3+ AI-assisted interactions in the week / Total active CSMs) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Not worth tracking formally. You likely have one or two CSMs and adoption is a personal choice at this stage. Focus on piloting one use case first.
why_priority.walk: Start measuring once you've rolled out an AI tool to the team. Low adoption signals the tool isn't solving a real pain or CSMs need more support.
why_priority.run: High-priority input metric. If AI-assisted resolution rate is high but adoption rate is low, a small group is carrying the AI program; that's fragile and doesn't scale.

common_mistakes: Measuring any AI tool usage instead of approved tools. Shadow IT usage inflates the number while hiding compliance and PII risks
Setting adoption targets without removing barriers first. If the tool is slow, poorly integrated, or adds steps, forcing adoption just breeds resentment
Treating 100% adoption as the goal without checking whether AI is actually helping. Compliance adoption is worse than no adoption
Not segmenting by CSM tenure. Newer CSMs typically adopt AI faster; senior CSMs may need different use cases to see value

audience.relevance: Track weekly during rollout, monthly once stable. Low adoption in a specific team often signals a training or tooling fit issue.
Use as a leading indicator for AI program ROI. Adoption rate and resolution rate together tell the full story.
Own the measurement methodology. Define 'active use' clearly, instrument it in your CS platform or HRIS, and report monthly.

metrics.name: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)
metrics.definition: The total revenue a business expects to generate from a single customer account over the entire duration of the relationship. CLV is both a backward-looking measure of realized value and a forward-looking model for deciding which customers warrant deeper investment.
metrics.formula: Average Annual Contract Value × Average Customer Lifespan (years)

why_priority.crawl: Too early to model reliably. You don't have enough retention history for CLV estimates to be accurate. Focus on keeping customers alive before projecting their value.
why_priority.walk: Useful as a strategic framing exercise, but don't optimize for it yet. Churn and expansion patterns aren't stable enough for CLV to drive decisions reliably.
why_priority.run: Start building CLV models by segment. Use them to justify CS headcount investment, inform tiering decisions, and prioritize accounts for high-touch coverage.

common_mistakes: Using a single blended CLV number across all segments. SMB and Enterprise CLV are structurally different and should never be averaged together
Treating CLV as a finance-only metric. CS directly influences lifespan and expansion — CLV should live on the CS dashboard, not just the board deck
Confusing CLV with ARR. CLV accounts for the full customer relationship including expansion, contraction, and eventual churn; ARR is a point-in-time snapshot
Building CLV models before you have reliable churn data. Inaccurate retention inputs produce CLV estimates that mislead rather than inform

audience.relevance: Use CLV to validate your growth model. If CLV is not meaningfully higher than CAC, scaling sales spend destroys value rather than creates it.
CLV shapes how you segment customers, allocate CS resources, and set expansion targets. High-CLV segments deserve disproportionate investment.
Use CLV by cohort to assess whether your CS motions are extending customer lifespan and growing account value. Segment-level CLV trends reveal where your team's time has the highest leverage.

metrics.name: Net Promoter Score (NPS)
metrics.definition: A measure of customer loyalty and likelihood to recommend, based on a single question: 'How likely are you to recommend us to a colleague or peer?' Scored 0–10, NPS splits respondents into Promoters (9–10), Passives (7–8), and Detractors (0–6). The result is a single score from -100 to +100.
metrics.formula: % Promoters (9–10) − % Detractors (0–6)
metrics.formula_note:
% Promoters = (Number of promoters / Total respondents) × 100
% Detractors = (Number of detractors / Total respondents) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Not a priority yet. You likely lack the survey volume for statistically meaningful results, and the real issue is whether customers are getting value at all. Start with CSAT at key touchpoints instead.
why_priority.walk: Start running quarterly relationship NPS surveys. The goal is building a baseline, not optimizing the score. Even basic detractor follow-up at this stage prevents preventable churn.
why_priority.run: NPS should be a core CS metric. Segment by ARR tier, cohort, and product line. Use detractor workflows to protect renewal pipeline; use promoter identification to feed expansion and reference programs.

common_mistakes: Running NPS annually. Annual surveys are too lagging to be actionable for CSMs managing accounts in real time. Quarterly relationship surveys are the minimum cadence
Collecting NPS without a detractor follow-up workflow. NPS data that sits in a dashboard without triggering outreach is just vanity — detractors who don't hear back churn at higher rates

Conflating relationship NPS with transactional NPS. Relationship NPS (quarterly, account-wide) measures loyalty; transactional NPS (post-interaction) measures a specific experience. Don't blend the scores
Using NPS as a health score input before you have reliable survey frequency. Quarterly or less frequent responses add noise to health models rather than signal

audience.relevance: Follow up with detractors within 48 hours of a low score. Promoters are your best expansion and reference candidates — identify them and act on it.
Segment NPS by ARR tier, cohort, and CSM team to distinguish systemic issues from isolated account problems. NPS trend direction matters more than the absolute score.
NPS is a leading indicator of renewal risk and expansion potential. Sustained NPS decline across a segment should trigger a strategic review, not just a CSM call.

metrics.name: Annual Recurring Revenue (ARR)
metrics.definition: The total annualized recurring revenue from all active customers. ARR represents the predictable revenue a business expects to earn from its current customer base over a year. For customer success teams, ARR is the core revenue base they help maintain and grow. They focus on retaining this revenue and expanding it within the customers they manage. Many teams also track managed ARR, which is the portion of ARR tied to the accounts they directly own, giving a clearer view of their impact.
metrics.formula: Sum of all active subscription values annualized (MRR × 12 for monthly billing)

why_priority.crawl: ARR visibility is useful for sizing the problem, but without GRR and logo retention under control, ARR growth will mask underlying churn risk. Focus on retention fundamentals before optimizing for ARR growth.
why_priority.walk: Begin tracking ARR by segment and cohort. CS should be able to report the ARR at risk and the ARR protected each quarter as a measure of team impact.
why_priority.run: ARR is a core reporting metric for CS. Segment-level ARR trends, ARR at risk, and ARR influenced by CS motions should feed directly into QBRs and board reporting.

seg_smb.good: 15–25% net ARR growth from existing customers annually

seg_midmarket.good: 20–30% net ARR growth from existing customers annually

seg_enterprise.good: 15–25% net ARR growth from existing customers annually

common_mistakes: Tracking total company ARR without segmenting by CS-managed accounts — without that split, you can't measure CS's actual impact on the revenue base
Confusing ARR with revenue recognised in the P&L. ARR is contracted recurring revenue, not cash collected or revenue recognised under accounting standards
Using ARR as a standalone metric without pairing it with NRR or GRR. Absolute ARR can grow while the quality of the base deteriorates through logo churn masked by large expansions
Conflating company ARR with CS-managed ARR. The portion your team owns differs from the total, and reporting the wrong number overstates or understates CS's footprint

audience.relevance: Know the ARR of every account in your book. ARR tier drives prioritisation — higher ARR accounts typically warrant more proactive engagement and faster escalation response.
Report ARR at risk, ARR protected, and expansion ARR each quarter. ARR segmented by health tier shows where the revenue base is most vulnerable.
ARR is the board-level measure of CS's stewardship of the revenue base. Tie CS headcount and investment decisions to managed ARR ratios.

metrics.name: Monthly Recurring Revenue (MRR)
metrics.definition: The total recurring revenue from all active customers, measured on a monthly basis. MRR represents the predictable revenue a business expects to earn each month from its current customer base. For customer success teams, MRR is useful for tracking short-term changes, especially in monthly billing cycles or high-velocity customer segments. Many teams also track managed MRR, which is the portion of MRR tied to the accounts they directly handle, giving a more detailed view of their impact.
metrics.formula: Sum of all active subscription values on a monthly basis (ARR ÷ 12 for annual contracts)

why_priority.crawl: MRR reporting adds little value before your retention fundamentals are in place. Focus on understanding why customers churn before optimizing the revenue cadence you report at.
why_priority.walk: Start tracking MRR movement in four categories: new, expansion, contraction, and churned. Even basic monthly visibility into contraction MRR will surface at-risk accounts earlier than renewal-date monitoring alone.
why_priority.run: MRR movement analysis should be a standard CS leadership metric. Contraction MRR trending upward is an early warning sign before logo churn hits. Use it to trigger proactive playbooks.

seg_smb.good: 1–2% net MRR growth from existing customers month-over-month

seg_midmarket.good: 1.5–2.5% net MRR growth from existing customers month-over-month

seg_enterprise.good: 1–2% net MRR growth from existing customers month-over-month

common_mistakes: Mixing annual and monthly contracts in MRR calculations without normalising — an annual contract must be divided by 12, not counted at full value in the month it closes
Tracking total MRR without breaking it into movement categories (new, expansion, contraction, churned). Without the breakdown, you can't tell whether changes are CS-driven or acquisition-driven

Treating MRR and ARR as interchangeable when billing models differ. Usage-based or seasonal components cause MRR to fluctuate in ways that annual figures smooth over — know which is more representative for your business
Conflating company MRR with CS-managed MRR. The portion your team owns is what CS performance should be measured against, not the total company figure

audience.relevance: MRR per account is a quick proxy for prioritisation. Contraction MRR — downgrades or seat reductions — is often the first financial signal of an at-risk account, showing up before the renewal conversation begins.

Track MRR movement by category monthly. Contraction MRR trending upward is an early warning sign before logo churn appears in the numbers. Segment by cohort and CSM to find where the pressure is concentrated.
MRR provides the highest-frequency view of CS's revenue impact. For monthly-billing or high-velocity SMB businesses, MRR is more actionable than ARR for in-quarter course corrections.

metrics.name: Annual Contract Value (ACV)
metrics.definition: The average annualized value of a customer contract, including all contracted fees — recurring, professional services, and implementation. Unlike ARR, which counts only recurring revenue across the full customer base, ACV is a per-contract metric used to size individual deals and compare them across cohorts or segments. CS teams use ACV to prioritise accounts, calibrate coverage models, and set expectations for the depth of engagement each account warrants.
metrics.formula: Total contract value ÷ contract length in years (for multi-year deals), or total annual fees for single-year contracts

why_priority.crawl: ACV awareness is useful for rough account prioritisation, but without a defined coverage model, it's hard to act on. Focus on understanding which accounts are at risk before optimising around deal size.
why_priority.walk: Use ACV to begin segmenting your book and calibrating CSM coverage ratios. High-ACV accounts typically warrant dedicated CSMs; lower ACV accounts may suit pooled or digital-led models.
why_priority.run: ACV should inform your coverage model, QBR cadence, and escalation thresholds. Segment your CS team's activities and capacity planning directly against ACV tiers.

common_mistakes: Conflating ACV with ARR. ACV is a per-contract metric that can include non-recurring fees; ARR is a portfolio metric counting only recurring revenue. Using them interchangeably distorts both coverage planning and board reporting
Building coverage models on headcount alone without anchoring to ACV tiers. A CSM managing 50 accounts at $5K ACV has a fundamentally different job to one managing 20 accounts at $100K ACV
Calculating ACV inconsistently across teams — some include professional services, others exclude them. Standardise the definition before using ACV for cross-team benchmarking or capacity planning
Ignoring ACV compression over time. If average ACV is declining across cohorts, it may signal a shift in your customer profile or pricing that CS needs to flag to leadership before it affects NRR

audience.relevance: ACV is the clearest signal of how much time and effort an account should receive. Know the ACV tier of every account in your book and use it to set expectations for engagement depth and response time.
Use ACV to build and defend your coverage model. CSM-to-account ratios, QBR cadence, and pooled vs. dedicated coverage decisions should all be anchored to ACV tiers, not headcount instinct.

ACV mix across the portfolio signals whether CS capacity investment is aligned to revenue concentration. If 20% of accounts represent 80% of ACV, coverage and risk management should reflect that asymmetry.

metrics.name: Customer Success Qualified Leads (CSQLs)
metrics.definition: A CS-identified expansion or upsell opportunity that meets agreed qualification criteria (product fit, usage signals, stakeholder buy-in, budget) and is handed to Sales as a qualified lead. CSQLs measure how reliably CS converts customer relationship intelligence into incremental pipeline, and they are the bridge between adoption work and revenue outcomes. Unlike Marketing Qualified Leads, CSQLs originate from inside the installed base and rely on CSM judgement about timing, fit, and account readiness.
metrics.formula: Count of CSQLs accepted by Sales in period. Secondary measure: CSQL conversion rate = (Closed-won CSQLs / Total CSQLs accepted) × 100

why_priority.crawl: Skip this metric for now. CSQLs require a stable retention base and a working CS-to-Sales handoff, neither of which is in place at this stage. Focus on adoption and renewal first.
why_priority.walk: Begin informally tracking CS-sourced expansion ideas, but do not formalise targets. Use the data to identify which usage and relationship signals tend to precede genuine expansion.
why_priority.run: CSQLs should be a formal metric with shared qualification criteria between CS and Sales, a documented handoff process, and weekly or monthly reporting on volume and conversion.

seg_smb.good: 2-4 CSQLs per CSM per quarter, 20-30% conversion

seg_midmarket.good: 3-5 CSQLs per CSM per quarter, 25-35% conversion

seg_enterprise.good: 1-3 CSQLs per CSM per quarter, 30-40% conversion

common_mistakes: No shared qualification definition with Sales. CS submits leads, Sales rejects them as unqualified, and trust between the teams erodes
Counting any usage uptick or positive signal as a CSQL. Without budget, stakeholder, and timing checks, conversion rates collapse and pipeline becomes noisy
Comping CSMs on CSQL volume alone. This incentivises low-quality submissions; tie at least part of the incentive to closed-won conversion or CSQL acceptance rate
No closed-loop reporting back to CS. If CSMs never learn which CSQLs converted and why, the qualification criteria never improve
Treating CSQLs as the only expansion path. Some accounts expand product-led or via direct Sales outreach; CSQLs should complement those motions, not replace them

audience.relevance: Know the qualification checklist cold and use it to filter your own account observations before submitting. A high acceptance rate is more valuable to your reputation with Sales than raw CSQL volume.
Track CSQL volume and conversion by CSM to identify coaching opportunities. Look for patterns in which usage and relationship signals consistently produce closed-won outcomes.
Own the qualification criteria jointly with Sales leadership and review them quarterly. CSQL conversion rate is the single best signal of whether CS and Sales are operationally aligned on expansion.

Use CSQL contribution to total expansion pipeline as a measure of CS revenue impact. If CS-sourced pipeline converts at higher rates than other sources, it justifies further investment in CS capacity.

profile_modifiers.description: Conditions next-step recommendations by profile context. The recommendation engine uses emphasizes/deprioritizes arrays to re-rank or filter priority actions. Values are stable string identifiers matched against option-level tags added in Task 6.

executive_summary_templates.description: 1–2 sentence profile-specific context sentences for the Executive Summary, keyed by customer_segment then cs_team_size. Renderer skips gracefully when either key is absent.

seg_enterprise.team_solo: As an enterprise CS team of one, the accounts you manage are high-stakes and complex, but you have no coverage depth. Your immediate priority is making your processes explicit enough that you can manage them systematically rather than from memory.
seg_enterprise.team_1_3: With a small team serving enterprise accounts, a single CSM departure or renewal surge can overwhelm your capacity. Formalizing playbooks and handoff processes now reduces the dependency on any one person's institutional knowledge.
seg_enterprise.team_4_10: A team of 4–10 covering enterprise accounts is a common inflection point: large enough to specialize, but small enough that informal coordination still works for now. The processes you formalize in the next year will determine whether you scale smoothly or hit a wall when headcount doubles.
seg_enterprise.team_10plus: With a mature enterprise CS team, your leverage comes from playbook discipline and data infrastructure, not individual CSM effort. The gap between your best and average CSMs is the clearest signal of where your playbook and tooling investments should focus next.

seg_midmarket.team_solo: Running CS solo across a mid-market book means constantly triaging between reactive account work and the proactive investments that compound over time. A health signal that surfaces where to look, rather than waiting for customers to raise issues, is the single most valuable thing you can build now.
seg_midmarket.team_1_3: A small team covering mid-market accounts has enough volume to see patterns but not enough headcount to address every signal. A documented coverage model that makes triage explicit (not every account needs the same attention) is more valuable than adding headcount before that clarity exists.
seg_midmarket.team_4_10: At 4–10 people covering mid-market accounts, you have enough capacity to run real playbooks, but coordination costs start to erode execution quality if processes aren't made explicit. The teams that scale well from here document and measure their work before informal habits become bottlenecks.
seg_midmarket.team_10plus: A larger mid-market CS team surfaces the cost of inconsistency: CSMs handling similar accounts with different approaches produce unpredictable outcomes. At your scale, standards (consistent playbooks, shared health criteria, and coordinated coverage) compound more than individual CSM quality.

seg_smb.team_solo: Managing a large SMB book alone is a volume problem before it is a quality problem. Every customer touchpoint that does not require human judgment should be systematized. Your goal is to make the interactions that need you the ones that actually get you.
seg_smb.team_1_3: At SMB scale with a small team, account volumes are high and manual coverage does not scale. Building a digital-first motion, where the CS platform handles the routine and CSMs handle only what automation cannot, is the architecture that makes this sustainable.
seg_smb.team_4_10: A team of 4–10 managing SMB accounts has the headcount to specialize, but the risk is building a high-touch motion that your unit economics cannot sustain. The clarifying question is: which interventions actually move your retention numbers, and which are effort that does not compound?
seg_smb.team_10plus: With a large SMB CS team, the benchmark question is accounts-per-CSM: if that ratio is not improving year over year while outcomes hold, you are growing headcount linearly with revenue instead of building the leverage that makes CS a scalable function.

contextual_recommendations.description: Lookup table for stage + context specific guidance. Used by the site to render contextual panels.

seg_enterprise.engagement_model: High-touch: named CSM, regular cadence, strategic business reviews

seg_midmarket.engagement_model: Medium-touch: named CSM with digital supplements, quarterly reviews

seg_smb.engagement_model: Low-touch / digital-first: pooled CSM model, automated journeys, community-driven support

cs_cost_benchmarks.arr_under_5m: CS team costs often 10–20% of ARR. Over-investing early is recommended for long-term retention.
cs_cost_benchmarks.arr_5m_20m: Target 8–15% of ARR for CS team costs. Start building efficiency through tooling and playbooks.
cs_cost_benchmarks.arr_20m_100m: Target 5–10% of ARR. Expect to see economies of scale from digital CS and automation.
cs_cost_benchmarks.arr_over_100m: Target under 5–8% of ARR. Mature organizations achieve this through operational discipline and technology.

nice_to_have: Predictive churn probability (once predictive models are in production)
CS-attributed revenue (once revenue strategy is formalized)

phases.label: Assess
phases.description: Score your CS maturity across 8 domains. Understand your strengths, gaps, and stage.

phases.label: Execute
phases.description: Follow stage-specific playbooks. Focus on the highest-leverage moves for your current maturity.

phases.label: Transform
phases.description: Track your progress. Re-assess to validate growth. Cross into your next maturity stage.

crawl.title: Establish a Retention Baseline
crawl.objective: Create a single, trusted retention number your team can rally around and improve.
crawl.why: Without a baseline, you can't tell if CS is working. Most crawl-stage teams don't know their real churn rate. They track logos lost but miss contraction, downgrades, and late renewals. A clear baseline turns 'we think we're doing okay' into 'we know where we stand.'
steps: Audit your billing or subscription data for the last 12 months, identifying every churned, downgraded, and expanded account
Calculate Gross Revenue Retention (GRR) and Logo Retention for the trailing 12 months
Segment the results by cohort (signup quarter) and customer size if possible
Identify the top 3 churn reasons by reviewing cancellation notes, support tickets, and CSM feedback
Set a GRR target for the next quarter based on your current number (aim for 2–5% improvement)
Create a simple monthly dashboard (spreadsheet is fine) that tracks GRR, logo retention, and churned ARR
Share the baseline and target with your leadership team to create accountability
common_pitfalls: Using logo count instead of revenue-weighted retention: a churned enterprise account matters more than 10 lost SMB accounts
Excluding downgrades from churn calculations, which inflates your retention number
Setting an unrealistic target (e.g., jumping from 80% to 95% GRR in one quarter)

crawl.title: Build Your First Customer Segmentation
crawl.objective: Divide your customer base into 2–3 tiers so you can allocate CS resources where they drive the most impact.
crawl.why: At the crawl stage, every customer gets the same treatment, which means high-value accounts are under-served and low-value accounts consume disproportionate time. Segmentation is the foundation of every scalable CS motion. Without it, you can't build differentiated playbooks, set appropriate touch models, or make rational hiring decisions.
steps: Export your customer list with ARR/ACV, contract dates, and any available usage data
Rank customers by ARR and identify natural breakpoints for 2–3 tiers (e.g., Strategic, Core, Scale)
For each tier, define the engagement model: high-touch (named CSM), mid-touch (pooled), or tech-touch (digital)
Calculate CSM-to-account ratios for each tier: aim for 1:10–20 for high-touch, 1:30–50 for mid-touch
Document the expected touchpoint cadence for each tier (weekly, biweekly, monthly, quarterly)
Assign existing accounts to tiers and rebalance CSM portfolios accordingly
Review segmentation quarterly. Customers grow and shrink; tiers should reflect current value
common_pitfalls: Creating too many segments too early. Start with 2–3, not 5–6
Segmenting only by ARR without considering growth potential or strategic value
Not actually changing behavior after segmentation. Tiers without differentiated engagement are just labels

crawl.title: Create a Basic Onboarding Program
crawl.objective: Build a repeatable onboarding process that gets new customers to first value within a defined timeframe.
crawl.why: Onboarding is where you set or break expectations. Crawl-stage teams often treat every new customer differently: whoever happens to be available runs onboarding their own way. This creates inconsistent time-to-value, early churn risk, and no way to identify where the process breaks down. A documented onboarding program is your first real playbook.
steps: Define 'first value' for your product: the specific outcome that makes a customer say 'this is working'
Map the current onboarding steps your team follows (even if informal) and identify the typical duration
Create a standardized onboarding checklist with milestones: kickoff, configuration, training, first value achieved
Set a Time to First Value (TTFV) target based on your current average (aim to reduce by 20–30%)
Build a simple onboarding email sequence (3–5 emails) that reinforces key milestones and next steps
Create a handoff template from Sales to CS that captures deal context, success criteria, and key stakeholders
Track onboarding completion rate and TTFV for every new customer starting now
common_pitfalls: Making onboarding too long. If it takes 90 days to reach first value, customers lose patience and engagement drops
